Beyond the fiscal damage, the educational consequences were substantial. Mass dismissal of experienced school leaders created an institutional vacuum. CGO's analysis found that some replacement acting directors lacked required qualifications, including adequate educational backgrounds or necessary teaching experience. The three-member selection commissions that appointed replacements operated without published guidelines, scoring criteria, or conflict-of-interest safeguards. This degraded school-level governance quality precisely when Montenegro needed strong institutional capacity to implement curriculum reforms and respond to post-pandemic educational recovery needs.

The prosecution by the Special State Prosecutor's Office (SDT) is welcome from an education policy perspective for several reasons. First, it establishes the principle that education governance decisions carry legal consequences when they violate the law. Second, it validates what courts and civil society organizations like CGO had already documented -- that these dismissals were politically motivated and procedurally unlawful. Third, it may deter future ministers from treating school leadership positions as political spoils.

However, several concerns temper this positive assessment. The timing raises questions: CGO filed a criminal complaint in November 2022, and the SDT reportedly did not act on it for over three years. This delay undermines the deterrent effect and suggests that prosecution may be influenced by political cycles rather than purely legal considerations, especially given that the arrest coincided with broader operations in Podgorica and Budva. The opposition's criticism about disproportionate arrest procedures, while politically motivated, touches on a valid point about whether the manner of the arrest was calibrated to the nature of the offense.

From an EU alignment perspective, the case highlights a persistent weakness in Montenegro's education governance. Chapter 26 (Education and Culture) has been provisionally closed, and Montenegro adopted an ambitious Education Reform Strategy 2025-2035 in partnership with UNICEF and the European Commission. That strategy explicitly calls for improving education management and financing with clear accountability mechanisms. However, the fundamental legal framework still vests the minister with the power to elect and dismiss school directors. Without structural reform to this appointment mechanism, the Bratić episode could recur under any future government.

The September 2024 amendments to the General Law on Education addressed some organizational matters including school board composition and director appointment procedures, but they did not fundamentally shift the balance of power away from the minister. EU best practice, as reflected in countries like Estonia, Finland, and Slovenia, typically places director selection authority with school boards or local governance bodies, with the ministry retaining oversight rather than direct appointment power.

For brain drain considerations, episodes of political instability in education send a troubling signal to young professionals considering careers in teaching or school administration in Montenegro. When school leaders can be dismissed en masse based on which government appointed them, it undermines the professional attractiveness of educational leadership -- already a challenge in a country of 620,000 people competing with EU labor markets for talent.

The broader lesson from this case is that accountability after the fact, while necessary, is no substitute for structural safeguards that prevent abuse in the first place. Montenegro needs governance reforms that make it structurally impossible for any single minister to unilaterally replace the leadership of an entire national school system.
key_concerns: The systemic vulnerability that enabled Bratić's mass dismissals remains: the minister still holds legal authority to elect and dismiss school directors, meaning a future minister could repeat this pattern.

The three-year delay between CGO's criminal complaint (November 2022) and the actual arrest (February 2026) raises concerns about politically influenced prosecution timing and weakens the deterrent effect.

Over 280,000 euros in compensation and court costs were diverted from the education budget, with additional damage claims still pending, directly reducing resources available for education quality improvements.

The appointment of unqualified acting directors during 2021-2022 caused lasting damage to school-level governance, and the full impact on education quality during that period has not been formally assessed.
The political polarization surrounding the arrest risks turning education governance reform into a partisan issue rather than a consensus-driven institutional improvement.
recommendations: Amend the General Law on Education to transfer director selection authority from the minister to school boards composed of parent, teacher, and municipal representatives, with the ministry retaining a supervisory and veto role for legal compliance only.

Commission an independent audit of the educational impact of the 2021-2022 mass dismissals, including effects on student outcomes, teacher turnover, and institutional continuity, to inform future governance safeguards.

Establish a transparent, merit-based director appointment process with published selection criteria, scored evaluations, and mandatory qualification thresholds, as recommended by CGO and aligned with EU best practices.
Create a dedicated budget line to recover funds lost to unlawful dismissal compensation and ring-fence those amounts for direct education quality investments such as teacher professional development.

Accelerate implementation of the accountability mechanisms outlined in the Education Reform Strategy 2025-2035, specifically those relating to management quality and institutional governance at the school level.
Introduce a statutory cooling-off period following a change of government during which school director positions cannot be altered except for documented cause, preventing wholesale political purges.

counter_proposal.title: School Governance Independence Act
counter_proposal.summary: Rather than relying solely on post-hoc criminal prosecution to address political interference in school management, the Ministry of Education would pursue comprehensive legislative reform that structurally prevents any minister from unilaterally dismissing school directors. The core change would transfer director appointment and dismissal authority to autonomous school boards while establishing an independent Education Governance Commission to oversee appointment processes and adjudicate disputes, removing the minister from direct operational control of school leadership.
key_changes: Transfer director appointment and dismissal authority from the minister to school boards, with appointments based on open competition, published criteria, and scored evaluations.

Establish an independent Education Governance Commission (three members nominated by the Parliament, teacher unions, and parent associations) empowered to review and annul irregular appointments or dismissals.
Introduce mandatory qualification standards for school directors including minimum management training, years of pedagogical experience, and professional certification.

Create a statutory protection period of 18 months following any change of government during which director positions cannot be altered except through documented performance-based cause adjudicated by the Education Governance Commission.
Mandate annual public reporting on school director appointments, dismissals, and performance evaluations across all 243 public educational institutions.
expected_benefits: Structurally prevents future mass political purges of school directors regardless of which party holds government.
Saves the education budget from costly litigation by ensuring lawful appointment procedures are followed from the outset.
Improves school leadership quality by enforcing merit-based selection and mandatory qualifications.
Strengthens Montenegro's EU accession profile by aligning with European governance standards for education institutions.
Enhances professional attractiveness of school leadership positions, supporting brain drain mitigation by offering career stability.
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: Moderately feasible. The legal framework can be amended through the Parliament, and there is precedent in the September 2024 amendments to the General Law on Education. The Education Reform Strategy 2025-2035 already identifies governance improvement as a strategic goal. Political resistance is likely from parties that benefit from ministerial appointment power, but CGO, UNICEF, and the European Commission have all advocated for depoliticized school governance, providing external pressure and technical support. Implementation would require 12-18 months for legislative drafting, consultation, and transition.
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Umjesto da se oslanja isključivo na naknadno krivično gonjenje kako bi se riješilo pitanje političkog miješanja u upravljanje školama, Ministarstvo prosvjete bi sprovelo sveobuhvatnu zakonodavnu reformu koja strukturalno sprječava bilo kojeg ministra da jednostrano razrješava direktore škola. Ključna promjena podrazumijevala bi prenos nadležnosti za imenovanje i razrješavanje direktora na autonomne školske odbore, uz osnivanje nezavisne Komisije za upravljanje obrazovanjem koja bi nadzirala postupke imenovanja i rješavala sporove, čime bi ministar bio uklonjen iz neposredne operativne kontrole nad rukovodstvom škola.
key_changes_mne: Prenijeti nadležnost imenovanja i razrješavanja direktora sa ministra na školske odbore, pri čemu bi imenovanja bila zasnovana na javnom konkursu, objavljenim kriterijumima i bodovanim ocjenama.

Osnovati nezavisnu Komisiju za upravljanje obrazovanjem (tri člana koja nominuju Skupština, sindikati nastavnika i udruženja roditelja), ovlašćenu da preispituje i poništava nepravilna imenovanja ili razrješenja.
Uvesti obavezne kvalifikacione standarde za direktore škola, uključujući minimalne obuke iz menadžmenta, godine pedagoškog iskustva i profesionalne sertifikate.

Uspostaviti zakonski zaštitni period od 18 mjeseci nakon svake promjene Vlade, tokom kojeg pozicije direktora ne mogu biti mijenjane osim na osnovu dokumentovanih razloga vezanih za učinak, o kojima odlučuje Komisija za upravljanje obrazovanjem.
Obavezati na godišnje javno izvještavanje o imenovanjima, razrješenjima i ocjenama učinka direktora škola u svih 243 javnih obrazovnih ustanova.
expected_benefits_mne: Strukturalno sprječava buduće masovne političke čistke direktora škola, bez obzira na to koja stranka je na vlasti.
Čuva budžet obrazovanja od skupih sudskih sporova osiguravanjem zakonitih postupaka imenovanja od samog početka.
Poboljšava kvalitet rukovodstva škola primjenom selekcije zasnovane na zaslugama i obaveznih kvalifikacija.
Jača profil Crne Gore u procesu pristupanja EU usklađivanjem s evropskim standardima upravljanja obrazovnim institucijama.
Povećava profesionalnu privlačnost rukovodećih pozicija u školama, doprinoseći ublažavanju odliva mozgova nuđenjem karijernog stabilnosti.
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Umjereno izvodljivo. Zakonski okvir može se izmijeniti putem Skupštine, a postoji presedan u izmjenama Opšteg zakona o obrazovanju iz septembra 2024. godine. Strategija reforme obrazovanja 2025–2035. već prepoznaje unapređenje upravljanja kao strateški cilj. Politički otpor je vjerovatnan od strana koje imaju koristi od ministarskog ovlašćenja imenovanja, ali su CGO, UNICEF i Evropska komisija zagovarali depolitizaciju upravljanja školama, pružajući spoljni pritisak i tehničku podršku. Implementacija bi zahtijevala 12–18 mjeseci za izradu zakonodavnih prijedloga, konsultacije i tranziciju.

assessments.summary_mne: Hapšenje bivše ministarke prosvjete Vesne Bratić zbog zloupotrebe službenog položaja predstavlja zakasnio, ali neophodan korak ka odgovornosti za jednu od najdestabilizujućih epizoda u nedavnoj istoriji crnogorskog obrazovanja. Masovno razrješenje više od 140 direktora škola 2021. godine, koje su sudovi proglasili nezakonitim u 130 okončanih postupaka, izazvalo je ozbiljnu institucionalnu nestabilnost, koštalo državni budžet više od 280.000 eura na ime odštete i odvuklo pažnju od suštinskih obrazovnih reformi. Iako krivično gonjenje šalje signal da političko miješanje u upravljanje školama nosi posljedice, Ministarstvo prosvjete napominje da samo hapšenje ne otklanja sistemske propuste u upravljanju koji su takvu zloupotrebu i omogućili, niti se njime nadoknađuje trajna šteta nanesena stabilnosti školskog rukovodstva, moralu nastavnog osoblja i povjerenju javnosti u obrazovne institucije.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Krivično gonjenje bivše ministarke Bratić pozitivan je, ali nedovoljan korak: njime se uspostavlja odgovornost za nezakonito masovno razrješenje više od 140 direktora škola, koje je Crnoj Gori koštalo više od 280.000 eura i destabilizovalo obrazovni sistem. Ključna briga ostaje činjenica da sistemska ranjivost nije otklonjena — ministar i dalje ima zakonsku mogućnost da jednostrano razrješava direktore škola. Ministarstvo prosvjete preporučuje da se ova sudska mjera upari sa strukturnom reformom upravljanja kojom bi se nadležnost za imenovanje direktora prenijela na nezavisne školske odbore, u skladu sa Strategijom reforme obrazovanja 2025–2035.
key_concerns_mne: Sistemska ranjivost koja je Bratićevoj omogućila masovna razrješenja i dalje postoji: ministar i dalje ima zakonsko ovlašćenje da bira i razrješava direktore škola, što znači da bi budući ministar mogao ponoviti isti obrazac.

Trogodišnje kašnjenje između krivične prijave CGO-a (novembar 2022.) i samog hapšenja (februar 2026.) pokrenulo je pitanja o politički motivisanom tajmingu krivičnog gonjenja i slabi odvraćajući efekat.

Više od 280.000 eura namijenjenih odštetama i sudskim troškovima preusmjereno je iz obrazovnog budžeta, uz dodatne zahtjeve za naknadu štete koji su i dalje u toku, čime se direktno smanjuju resursi dostupni za unapređenje kvaliteta obrazovanja.

Postavljanje nekvalifikovanih vršilaca dužnosti direktora tokom 2021–2022. godine nanijelo je trajnu štetu upravljanju na nivou škola, a cjelokupan uticaj na kvalitet obrazovanja u tom periodu nije formalno procijenjen.
Politička polarizacija oko hapšenja prijeti da reformu upravljanja obrazovanjem pretvori u stranačko pitanje, umjesto da je tretira kao konsenzusno institucionalno poboljšanje.
recommendations_mne: Izmijeniti Opšti zakon o obrazovanju kako bi se nadležnost za izbor direktora prenijela sa ministra na školske odbore sastavljene od predstavnika roditelja, nastavnog osoblja i lokalne samouprave, pri čemu bi ministarstvo zadržalo isključivo nadzornu i pravo veta radi provjere zakonitosti.

Naručiti nezavisnu reviziju obrazovnih posljedica masovnih razrješenja iz 2021–2022. godine, uključujući efekte na postignuća učenika, fluktuaciju nastavnog osoblja i institucionalnu kontinuitet, kako bi se informisale buduće mjere zaštite u upravljanju.

Uspostaviti transparentan postupak imenovanja direktora zasnovan na stručnosti, s objavljenim kriterijumima selekcije, bodovanim ocjenama i obaveznim pragovima kvalifikacija, u skladu s preporukama CGO-a i najboljim praksama EU.

Formirati posebnu budžetsku stavku za povraćaj sredstava izgubljenih na ime naknada zbog nezakonitih razrješenja i ta sredstva rezervisati isključivo za direktna ulaganja u kvalitet obrazovanja, poput stručnog usavršavanja nastavnika.

Ubrzati implementaciju mehanizama odgovornosti predviđenih Strategijom reforme obrazovanja 2025–2035, posebno onih koji se odnose na kvalitet upravljanja i institucionalnu organizaciju na nivou škola.

Uvesti zakonski propisan period mirovanja nakon promjene vlasti, tokom kojeg pozicije direktora škola ne mogu biti mijenjane osim u slučaju dokumentovanog razloga, kako bi se spriječilo paušalno političko smjenjivanje kadrova.

assessments.summary: The arrest of former Education Minister Vesna Bratić by the Special State Prosecutor's Office represents a legitimate exercise of prosecutorial authority in pursuing abuse of office charges, which is consistent with Montenegro's EU accession obligations under Chapters 23 and 24 to build a credible anti-corruption track record. However, the manner of execution -- the use of handcuffs, the spectacle of the arrest, and the initial 72-hour detention for what is fundamentally a non-violent administrative offense involving disputed ministerial decisions -- raises serious proportionality concerns from a law enforcement standards perspective. The operation delivered a mixed message: it demonstrated willingness to hold former officials accountable, but the heavy-handed approach undermined public trust in proportionate policing and gave ammunition to opposition forces claiming selective and politically motivated prosecution.
assessments.executive_summary: The Bratić arrest is procedurally legitimate but operationally disproportionate. While holding former officials accountable aligns with EU Chapter 23/24 benchmarks, the spectacle of handcuffing a non-violent suspect for an administrative offense eroded public trust in law enforcement proportionality. The Ministry of Interior recommends adopting formal guidelines on the use of coercive measures during arrests of non-violent suspects to prevent politicization of law enforcement actions.
assessments.reasoning:
From the Ministry of Interior's perspective, this case raises several critical issues at the intersection of law enforcement practice, public trust, and EU integration requirements.

LEGAL BASIS AND PROSECUTORIAL AUTHORITY: The Special State Prosecutor's Office (SDT) acted within its statutory mandate. Abuse of official position is a criminal offense under Montenegrin law, and the SDT has jurisdiction over high-level corruption and abuse of power. The investigation had been ongoing since at least 2023, and the arrest was preceded by court rulings confirming the unlawfulness of Bratić's dismissals of over 140 school principals. The financial damage to the state -- estimated between EUR 280,000 and EUR 400,000 in compensation payments -- while not enormous by corruption standards, is not trivial. The legal foundation for the prosecution appears sound.

PROPORTIONALITY OF LAW ENFORCEMENT ACTION: This is the Ministry of Interior's primary concern. The arrest was executed by special police units, with Bratić placed in handcuffs and publicly transported. A 72-hour initial detention was followed by a 30-day pretrial detention order. For an offense that is fundamentally administrative in nature -- a former minister making disputed personnel decisions -- the deployment of these measures raises serious proportionality questions. President Milatović himself publicly noted that the treatment of "a university professor" appeared disproportionate compared to how persons accused of far more serious crimes have been treated. The Higher Court subsequently agreed, replacing detention with house arrest and electronic monitoring. This correction by the judiciary suggests the initial police and prosecutorial approach exceeded what was warranted by the circumstances.

SELECTIVE ENFORCEMENT CONCERNS: The timing of the arrest, coinciding with operations against Blažo Šaranović (former Property Administration director suspected of EUR 9 million in damages), created an appearance of coordinated action. While bundling operations is standard practice, the Bratić case -- involving far smaller alleged damages and a non-violent administrative matter -- benefited from the dramatic optics of being paired with a larger corruption case. The opposition's criticism, including Parliament Speaker Mandić's statement that Bratić was "treated like a common criminal while the top of the criminal octopus walks freely," reflects a broader public perception problem. Whether or not these perceptions are accurate, the Ministry of Interior must recognize that law enforcement credibility depends on consistent and proportionate application of coercive measures across all cases, regardless of the suspect's political affiliation.

EU ACCESSION IMPLICATIONS (CHAPTERS 23 AND 24): Montenegro's EU accession process requires a demonstrable track record of fighting corruption and holding public officials accountable. The 2025 EU Progress Report noted that Montenegro has "an initial track record in the fight against high-level corruption" but "limited results in some areas." The action plan deadlines for Chapters 23 and 24 final benchmarks are set for Q3 2026. In this context, the Bratić prosecution can be seen as contributing to that track record. However, the EU also evaluates the quality of prosecutions and respect for fundamental rights. A case that the EU or Council of Europe later flags as disproportionate could become a negative benchmark rather than a positive one. The European Court of Human Rights jurisprudence on proportionality of pretrial detention is clear: detention must be justified by specific risks (flight, witness tampering, severity) that cannot be addressed by less restrictive measures.

IMPACT ON POLICE CREDIBILITY AND PUBLIC TRUST: The police acted on prosecutorial orders, which is their legal obligation. However, the manner of execution -- especially the visible use of handcuffs -- is within police operational discretion. Montenegro lacks clear, codified guidelines on the use of coercive measures during arrests of non-violent suspects. This gap allows for inconsistent treatment and exposes police to accusations of political instrumentalization. The fact that both governing coalition allies and opposition parties criticized the arrest procedure is notable -- it suggests the proportionality concern transcends political lines. From the Ministry of Interior's perspective, this case exposed a vulnerability in police operational protocols that needs to be addressed through formal guidelines.

ADMINISTRATIVE CAPACITY DIMENSION: The underlying case itself reflects a broader administrative capacity weakness. A minister made unlawful personnel decisions affecting 140 individuals, courts reversed those decisions, and the state bore the financial consequences. The Ministry of Interior's administrative police and inspection functions depend on a well-functioning civil service system. When ministers can unilaterally dismiss large numbers of officials -- whether legally or illegally -- it destabilizes institutional capacity across all sectors, including interior affairs. The prosecution, whatever its procedural flaws, addresses this underlying governance problem.

CONCLUSION: The decision to prosecute Bratić is defensible on legal and EU integration grounds. The decision to arrest her with special police units, apply handcuffs, and seek 30-day detention for a non-violent administrative offense was disproportionate and counterproductive. It politicized what should have been a straightforward accountability process and gave credible ammunition to those claiming selective justice. The subsequent judicial correction (replacing detention with electronic monitoring) confirms the initial overreach. The Ministry of Interior should use this case as impetus to develop formal proportionality guidelines for arrest procedures.

Sources consulted: CdM reporting on the arrest and detention, Adria TV coverage of political reactions, OCCRP reporting on the simultaneous Šaranović arrest, the 2025 EU Rule of Law Report for Montenegro, the European Parliament report on Montenegro's Chapters 23 and 24 progress, and Balkan Insight coverage of Montenegro's prosecutorial track record.
key_concerns: Disproportionate use of coercive measures: Deployment of special police units, handcuffs, and 72-hour detention for a non-violent administrative abuse-of-office case exceeds what is warranted by the nature of the offense and the suspect's profile.

Absence of codified proportionality guidelines for police arrest procedures: Montenegro lacks formal, binding operational guidelines on when handcuffs and physical detention are justified versus a summons or voluntary surrender, creating inconsistency and vulnerability to political criticism.

Selective enforcement perception: The dramatic arrest of Bratić contrasts with more lenient treatment of suspects in cases involving far greater alleged damages and more serious offenses, fueling public perception of politically motivated prosecution.

Risk of negative EU benchmarking: While the prosecution contributes to Montenegro's Chapter 23/24 track record, disproportionate procedural treatment could be flagged by EU evaluators or the European Court of Human Rights as a fundamental rights concern, turning a positive into a negative.

Politicization of law enforcement: The arrest generated political solidarity rallies, criticism from the Parliament Speaker and President, and ethnic framing of the prosecution, all of which undermine the institutional neutrality that police and prosecutorial services must project.

Erosion of public trust in police neutrality: When the same police apparatus is perceived as treating different political actors differently, it damages the broader legitimacy of law enforcement operations, including those targeting organized crime and serious corruption.
recommendations: Develop and adopt formal Operational Guidelines on Proportionate Use of Coercive Measures during arrests, specifying criteria for when handcuffs, special police deployment, and physical detention are justified versus summons-based procedures, particularly for non-violent offenses.

Establish an internal review mechanism within the Police Directorate (Uprava policije) for post-operation assessment of arrest procedures in high-profile cases, ensuring compliance with proportionality standards before political or media fallout occurs.

Coordinate with the Special State Prosecutor's Office to develop joint protocols for the arrest of public officials and former officials, ensuring that the method of apprehension is calibrated to actual flight risk and danger assessments rather than case optics.

Request the Judicial Training Centre to conduct training sessions for judges, prosecutors, and police on European Court of Human Rights jurisprudence regarding pretrial detention proportionality, particularly in non-violent white-collar cases.

Publish anonymized statistics on arrest methods by offense type to demonstrate consistent application of coercive measures across all cases, which would help counter perceptions of selective enforcement.

Use this case to advocate for strengthening the civil service protection framework, ensuring that mass unlawful dismissals by any minister are prevented through administrative checks before they occur, rather than addressed solely through criminal prosecution after the fact.

counter_proposal.title: Proportionate Accountability Framework
counter_proposal.summary: The Ministry of Interior would support the prosecution of former Minister Bratić but would fundamentally change the method of apprehension and detention. Instead of a special police arrest with handcuffs, the ministry would advocate for a summons-based approach combined with preemptive travel restrictions, reserving physical arrest for cases where the suspect fails to comply or where a genuine flight risk is documented. Simultaneously, the ministry would launch a parallel administrative reform initiative to prevent similar mass unlawful dismissals from occurring in the future.
key_changes: Replace physical arrest with a court summons for non-violent abuse-of-office suspects who have no documented flight risk, with a 48-hour compliance window before escalation to warrant-based arrest.

Implement mandatory Proportionality Assessment Forms that police commanders must complete and submit before executing arrest warrants for non-violent offenses, documenting the justification for the level of force and coercion applied.

Establish a joint SDT-Police protocol requiring risk-based classification of suspects (flight risk, danger to public, evidence tampering risk) before determining the arrest method, removing discretion for politically sensitive spectacle arrests.

Create a proactive administrative mechanism through the Ministry of Public Administration requiring real-time legal review of mass personnel decisions by ministers before they take effect, preventing the accumulation of unlawful acts that later require criminal prosecution.

Mandate that pretrial detention requests for non-violent offenses be accompanied by a written justification explaining why less restrictive measures (travel ban, electronic monitoring, bail) are insufficient, reducing judicial rubber-stamping of detention requests.
expected_benefits: Elimination of the perception of selective and politically motivated law enforcement by establishing consistent, rule-based arrest procedures for all suspects regardless of political status.
Stronger EU Chapter 23/24 compliance by demonstrating that Montenegro respects both the accountability and fundamental rights dimensions of rule of law.
Reduced political fallout from necessary anti-corruption prosecutions, allowing the justice system to build credibility without generating solidarity rallies and parliamentary crises.
Prevention of future mass unlawful dismissals through administrative safeguards, reducing the need for expensive court cases and eventual criminal prosecutions.
Enhanced police institutional credibility by insulating officers from accusations of serving as instruments of political pressure.
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: High feasibility. The operational guidelines and proportionality assessment forms could be developed within 3-6 months using existing police training infrastructure and with technical assistance from EU TAIEX or Council of Europe programs. The joint SDT-Police protocol would require inter-institutional negotiation but faces no legal barriers. The administrative safeguard for mass personnel decisions would require amendments to the Law on Civil Servants but aligns with ongoing public administration reform commitments. Total implementation cost is minimal -- primarily staff time for drafting and training -- and the political benefit of depoliticizing law enforcement operations is substantial.
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Ministarstvo unutrašnjih poslova podržalo bi krivično gonjenje bivšeg ministra Bratića, ali bi suštinski promijenilo metod hapšenja i pritvora. Umjesto specijalnog policijskog hapšenja s lisicama, ministarstvo bi zagovaralo pristup zasnovan na sudskom pozivu u kombinaciji s preventivnim mjerama zabrane putovanja, rezervišući fizičko hapšenje za slučajeve u kojima osumnjičeni ne postupi po pozivu ili u kojima je stvarna opasnost od bijega dokumentovana. Istovremeno, ministarstvo bi pokrenulo paralelnu inicijativu za administrativne reforme kako bi se spriječila buduća masovna nezakonita razrješenja.
key_changes_mne: Zamijeniti fizičko hapšenje sudskim pozivom za osumnjičene za nenasilnu zloupotrebu položaja koji nemaju dokumentovanu opasnost od bijega, uz rok usaglašavanja od 48 sati prije eskalacije na hapšenje po nalogu.

Uvesti obavezne obrasce za procjenu proporcionalnosti koje komandiri policije moraju popuniti i dostaviti prije izvršenja naloga za hapšenje za nenasilna krivična djela, uz dokumentovanje opravdanosti nivoa primijenjene sile i prinude.

Uspostaviti zajednički protokol SDT-a i policije koji zahtijeva klasifikaciju osumnjičenih na osnovu procjene rizika (opasnost od bijega, opasnost po javnost, rizik od uticaja na dokaze) prije određivanja metoda hapšenja, čime se uklanja diskrecija pri politički osjetljivim hapšenjima radi javnog efekta.

Stvoriti proaktivni administrativni mehanizam putem Ministarstva javne uprave koji obavezuje na pravnu provjeru u realnom vremenu masovnih kadrovskih odluka ministara prije nego što stupe na snagu, čime se sprječava gomilanje nezakonitih radnji koje kasnije zahtijevaju krivično gonjenje.

Obavezati da zahtjevi za pritvor u predmetima nenasilnih krivičnih djela budu praćeni pisanim obrazloženjem kojim se pojašnjava zašto manje restriktivne mjere (zabrana putovanja, elektronski nadzor, kaucija) nisu dovoljne, čime se smanjuje mehaničko sudsko odobravanje zahtjeva za pritvor.
expected_benefits_mne: Otklanjanje percepcije selektivnog i politički motivisanog provođenja zakona uvođenjem dosljednih, pravilima zasnovanih postupaka hapšenja za sve osumnjičene, bez obzira na politički status.
Jače usklađivanje s poglavljima 23/24 EU demonstriranjem da Crna Gora poštuje i dimenziju odgovornosti i dimenziju temeljnih prava vladavine prava.
Smanjenje političkih posljedica neophodnih antikorupcijskih krivičnih gonjenja, što sistemu pravde omogućava izgradnju kredibiliteta bez izazivanja skupova solidarnosti i parlamentarnih kriza.
Sprečavanje budućih masovnih nezakonitih razrješenja kroz administrativne zaštitne mehanizme, čime se smanjuje potreba za skupim sudskim postupcima i eventualnim krivičnim gonjenjem.
Jačanje institucionalnog kredibiliteta policije zaštitom službenika od optužbi da služe kao instrumenti političkog pritiska.
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Visoka izvodljivost. Operativne smjernice i obrasci za procjenu proporcionalnosti mogli bi biti izrađeni u roku od 3 do 6 mjeseci korišćenjem postojeće infrastrukture za obuku policije i uz tehničku podršku programa EU TAIEX ili Savjeta Evrope. Zajednički protokol SDT-a i policije zahtijevao bi međuinstitucionalnu koordinaciju, ali se ne suočava ni sa kakvim pravnim preprekama. Administrativni zaštitni mehanizam za masovne kadrovske odluke zahtijevao bi izmjene Zakona o državnim službenicima, ali je usklađen s tekućim obavezama u okviru reforme javne uprave. Ukupni troškovi implementacije su minimalni — prevashodno se radi o angažovanju osoblja na izradi akata i obuci — a politička korist od depolitizacije policijskih operacija je značajna.

assessments.summary_mne: Hapšenje bivše ministarke prosvjete Vesne Bratić od strane Specijalnog državnog tužilaštva predstavlja legitimno ostvarivanje tužilačkih ovlašćenja u procesuiranju optužbi za zloupotrebu položaja, što je u skladu s obavezama Crne Gore u okviru poglavlja 23 i 24 u procesu pristupanja EU, kojima se nastoji izgraditi vjerodostojna evidencija u borbi protiv korupcije. Međutim, način izvršenja — upotreba lisica, spektakularnost hapšenja i početni pritvor od 72 sata za ono što je u suštini nenasilno administrativno krivično djelo koje uključuje sporna ministarijska rješenja — izaziva ozbiljne zabrinutosti u pogledu proporcionalnosti iz ugla standarda policijskog postupanja. Operacija je poslala miješanu poruku: pokazala je spremnost da se bivši funkcioneri pozovu na odgovornost, ali je pretjerano krut pristup narušio povjerenje javnosti u proporcionalno policijsko djelovanje i dao argumente opozicionim snagama koje tvrde da se radi o selektivnom i politički motivisanom gonjenju.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Hapšenje Bratićeve je proceduralno legitimno, ali operativno nesrazmjerno. Iako pozivanje bivših funkcionera na odgovornost odgovara referentnim vrijednostima EU u okviru poglavlja 23/24, spektakl stavljanja lisica nenasilnoj osumnjičenoj zbog administrativnog prekršaja narušio je povjerenje javnosti u proporcionalnost organa za sprovođenje zakona. Ministarstvo unutrašnjih poslova preporučuje usvajanje formalnih smjernica o primjeni prinudnih mjera tokom hapšenja nenasilnih osumnjičenih kako bi se spriječila politizacija postupanja organa za sprovođenje zakona.
key_concerns_mne: Nesrazmjerna primjena prinudnih mjera: Angažovanje specijalnih policijskih jedinica, upotreba lisica i pritvor od 72 sata u slučaju nenasilne administrativne zloupotrebe položaja prelaze granice onoga što je opravdano s obzirom na prirodu krivičnog djela i profil osumnjičene.

Odsustvo kodifikovanih smjernica o proporcionalnosti u policijskim postupcima hapšenja: Crna Gora nema formalne, obavezujuće operativne smjernice o tome kada su lisice i fizički pritvor opravdani u odnosu na poziv ili dobrovoljno predavanje, što stvara nedosljednost i ranjivost na političke kritike.

Percepcija selektivnog postupanja: Dramatično hapšenje Bratićeve kontrastira s blažim tretmanom osumnjičenih u predmetima koji uključuju daleko veće navodne štete i teža krivična djela, čime se hrani javna percepcija o politički motivisanom gonjenju.

Rizik od negativnih EU ocjena: Iako gonjenje doprinosi evidenciji Crne Gore u okviru poglavlja 23/24, nesrazmjerno procesno postupanje mogli bi označiti kao zabrinutost u pogledu osnovnih prava i EU evaluatori i Evropski sud za ljudska prava, pretvarajući pozitivan korak u negativan.

Politizacija organa za sprovođenje zakona: Hapšenje je izazvalo skupove solidarnosti, kritike predsjednika Skupštine i predsjednika države, kao i etničko kontekstualizovanje gonjenja — sve to podriva institucionalnu neutralnost koju policija i tužilaštvo moraju projektovati.

Narušavanje povjerenja javnosti u neutralnost policije: Kada se stekne utisak da isti policijski aparat različito postupa prema različitim političkim akterima, to nanosi štetu ukupnom legitimitetu organa za sprovođenje zakona, uključujući i one koji se bore protiv organizovanog kriminala i ozbiljne korupcije.
recommendations_mne: Izraditi i usvojiti formalne Operativne smjernice o proporcionalnoj primjeni prinudnih mjera tokom hapšenja, uz preciziranje kriterijuma za opravdanost upotrebe lisica, angažovanja specijalne policije i fizičkog pritvora u odnosu na postupke pozivanja, posebno za nenasilna krivična djela.

Uspostaviti interni mehanizam preispitivanja unutar Uprave policije za ocjenu postupaka hapšenja nakon operacije u predmetima od visokog javnog interesa, kako bi se obezbijedila usklađenost sa standardima proporcionalnosti prije nego što dođe do političkih ili medijskih posljedica.

Koordinisati sa Specijalnim državnim tužilaštvom radi razvoja zajedničkih protokola za hapšenje javnih funkcionera i bivših funkcionera, kako bi se metod privođenja uskladio sa stvarnom procjenom rizika bijega i opasnosti, a ne s medijskom slikom predmeta.

Zatražiti od Centra za obuku u pravosuđu organizovanje obuka za sudije, tužioce i policiju o sudskoj praksi Evropskog suda za ljudska prava u pogledu proporcionalnosti pritvora prije suđenja, posebno u nenasilnim predmetima bijelog okovratnika.

Objavljivati anonimizovane statistike o metodama hapšenja prema vrsti krivičnog djela kako bi se demonstrirala dosljedna primjena prinudnih mjera u svim predmetima, što bi pomoglo u suzbijanju percepcije o selektivnom postupanju.

Iskoristiti ovaj slučaj kao argument za jačanje okvira zaštite u državnoj službi, osiguravajući da se masovna nezakonita razrješenja od strane bilo kojeg ministra sprečavaju administrativnim kontrolama prije nego što se dogode, umjesto da se rješavaju isključivo krivičnim gonjenjem nakon što je šteta načinjena.

assessments.summary: The arrest of former Education Minister Vesna Bratic by the Special State Prosecutor's Office represents a meaningful step in building Montenegro's anti-corruption track record, which is a critical requirement for closing Chapters 23 and 24 and advancing toward EU membership. The prosecution of a former minister for abuse of office -- based on court-confirmed unlawful dismissals of over 140 school principals and over EUR 280,000 in state-paid damages -- sends a signal that high-level officials face accountability. However, the political backlash from opposition parties and Parliament Speaker Mandic, who publicly criticized the arrest procedure, risks undermining prosecutorial independence, which the EU Delegation has explicitly warned against. The net assessment is positive for EU integration, provided the prosecution proceeds on merit, free from political interference, and that proportionality concerns regarding the arrest procedure are addressed transparently.
assessments.executive_summary: This arrest is a positive signal for Montenegro's EU accession, directly strengthening the anti-corruption track record required under Chapters 23 and 24. The top concern is that political criticism of the arrest by the Parliament Speaker and opposition parties risks undermining prosecutorial autonomy -- precisely what the EU Delegation has warned against. The Ministry recommends that all political actors publicly affirm respect for prosecutorial independence and that the SPO ensure full procedural transparency to sustain credibility with Brussels.
assessments.reasoning:
The arrest of former Minister Bratic must be assessed against Montenegro's critical EU accession timeline. Montenegro received a positive Interim Benchmark Assessment Report (IBAR) for Chapters 23 and 24 in June 2024, and is now working toward three final benchmarks in each of these chapters, with a deadline of Q3 2026. As of January 2026, 13 of 33 chapters have been provisionally closed, but Chapters 23 and 24 remain open and are the gatekeepers of overall accession progress. The European Commission's 2025 Progress Report noted that while Montenegro's track record on high-level corruption investigations has improved, final convictions remain low and the perception of impunity persists. Against this backdrop, the SPO's decision to investigate and arrest a former cabinet minister for abuse of office is directly relevant to the final benchmarks. The underlying facts are well-established: courts confirmed that Bratic unlawfully dismissed over 140 school principals in 2021, that the Ministry itself sabotaged the board formation process it cited as justification for the dismissals, and that the state paid over EUR 280,000 in court-ordered compensation. This is not a speculative or politically motivated case -- it rests on confirmed judicial findings of unlawful conduct that caused measurable financial damage to the state.

From the perspective of Chapter 23 (Judiciary and Fundamental Rights), the prosecution demonstrates that the judicial system can hold former high-ranking officials accountable, a core requirement of the final benchmarks. The EU has consistently demanded a credible track record of investigations, prosecutions, and convictions in high-level corruption and abuse of office cases. Each successful prosecution of a former minister contributes to this track record. Moreover, this follows the December 2024 arrest of two former agriculture ministers on corruption charges, suggesting a pattern of increasing accountability rather than an isolated event.

However, several elements raise concern from an EU integration perspective. First, the political reaction has been troubling. Parliament Speaker Mandic and opposition parties NSD and SNP publicly criticized the arrest procedure, with NSD characterizing Bratic as being "led away like a hardened criminal." The EU Delegation to Montenegro has explicitly warned that political actors should refrain from statements that could be interpreted as attempts to influence prosecutorial decisions. Such public criticism of a prosecutorial action by the Speaker of Parliament -- the third-highest constitutional office -- risks being read by Brussels as exactly the kind of political pressure on judicial independence that the EU has flagged as problematic. This directly affects the assessment under final benchmarks for Chapter 23 regarding judicial and prosecutorial independence.

Second, the proportionality of the arrest procedure warrants scrutiny. While the SPO has discretion over operational methods, the use of arrest and 72-hour detention for what is essentially a case of administrative abuse (unlawful dismissals) rather than violent crime or flight risk may raise questions about proportionality under EU fundamental rights standards. If the arrest is perceived as disproportionate or politically motivated, it could undermine rather than strengthen Montenegro's rule of law credentials.

Third, the ethnicization of the case -- with mobilization of narratives portraying the arrest as anti-Serbian persecution -- threatens to poison the political environment and distract from the substantive rule-of-law questions. The EU accession process requires consensus-building and cross-party commitment to reform. Framing prosecutorial actions through ethnic lenses undermines this consensus.

Regarding Chapter 24 (Justice, Freedom and Security), the case touches on the effectiveness of the Special State Prosecutor's Office, which the EU has noted faces capacity and infrastructure constraints. The SPO's ability to bring complex abuse-of-office cases against former ministers demonstrates operational capacity, but the office needs adequate resources to see such cases through to conviction.

The case also has implications for Chapter 26 (Education and Culture), though this chapter is provisionally closed. The underlying conduct -- mass politically-motivated dismissals of school principals -- represents a serious interference with educational institutional autonomy. Ensuring such conduct faces consequences helps prevent recurrence and strengthens the integrity of the education system.

Finally, regarding IPA funding, the EU's Instrument for Pre-Accession Assistance has funded rule-of-law and judicial reform projects in Montenegro. Demonstrating that these investments are producing tangible accountability outcomes (i.e., that strengthened prosecutorial capacity is being used to address high-level abuse) supports continued and potentially increased IPA allocations for the 2021-2027 programming period. Conversely, if the case collapses or is perceived as politically selective, it could raise questions about the effectiveness of EU-funded institutional strengthening.

Sources consulted: CdM, Portal Analitika, Danas, Slobodna Evropa, European Commission 2025 Progress Report, EU Council press releases on chapter closures, EEAS statements on Montenegro, European Western Balkans analysis, Freedom House Montenegro reports.
key_concerns: Ch.23 Judiciary and Fundamental Rights -- Public criticism of the arrest by Parliament Speaker Mandic and opposition parties risks being interpreted by Brussels as political pressure on prosecutorial independence, directly contradicting EU Delegation warnings that political actors must refrain from influencing prosecutorial decisions.

Ch.23 Judiciary and Fundamental Rights -- Proportionality of the arrest procedure (72-hour detention for administrative abuse of office) may raise fundamental rights concerns under EU standards, potentially undermining the rule-of-law signal the prosecution is meant to send.

Ch.23 and Ch.24 -- The ethnicization of the case, with narratives framing the prosecution as anti-Serbian persecution, threatens to erode the cross-party consensus needed for EU accession reforms and could complicate the political environment for closing the final benchmarks by Q3 2026.

Ch.23 Judiciary and Fundamental Rights -- The SPO's track record still shows a gap between investigations and final convictions in high-level cases. If this prosecution does not result in a conviction, it could reinforce the perception of impunity that the EU Commission has flagged.

Ch.23 and Ch.24 -- The Special State Prosecutor's Office continues to face infrastructure and human resource constraints noted in the 2025 EU Progress Report, raising questions about whether it has capacity to simultaneously pursue multiple high-profile cases effectively.
recommendations: All political actors, especially Parliament Speaker Mandic, should publicly affirm respect for prosecutorial independence in line with explicit EU Delegation guidance. The Ministry of EU Integration should issue a statement reminding political stakeholders that statements perceived as pressuring the SPO directly jeopardize progress on Chapter 23 final benchmarks.

The SPO should proactively communicate the legal basis and proportionality justification for the arrest procedure, addressing public concerns transparently. This would strengthen rather than undermine the rule-of-law narrative with Brussels.

The Government should accelerate implementation of the April 2025 Action Plans for final benchmarks in Chapters 23 and 24, using this case as evidence of the strengthening accountability framework, while ensuring the case proceeds strictly on legal merits.

Additional budgetary and human resources should be allocated to the Special State Prosecutor's Office to address the capacity constraints identified in the 2025 EU Progress Report, ensuring it can effectively manage multiple high-profile cases simultaneously.

The Ministry of EU Integration should prepare a briefing for the European Commission documenting this prosecution as part of Montenegro's expanding track record on high-level accountability, while noting the institutional safeguards ensuring prosecutorial independence from political influence.

Cross-party dialogue should be facilitated to prevent the ethnicization of judicial proceedings, as this dynamic directly threatens the political stability and reform consensus required for EU accession progress.

counter_proposal.title: EU-Aligned Accountability Framework for High-Level Abuse of Office
counter_proposal.summary: Rather than relying solely on criminal prosecution with arrest procedures that invite proportionality criticism, the Ministry of EU Integration would advocate a comprehensive accountability framework combining criminal prosecution with administrative and civil recovery mechanisms, transparent communication, and explicit political consensus on non-interference -- all structured to maximize positive assessment under Chapters 23 and 24 final benchmarks.
key_changes: Ensure proportional use of coercive measures: summons rather than arrest for non-violent abuse of office cases where there is no flight risk, reserving arrest for cases where it is demonstrably necessary under European Court of Human Rights jurisprudence.

Establish a formal Government protocol requiring all political officeholders (including the Parliament Speaker) to issue standardized statements of non-interference when prosecutorial actions target former officials, aligned with EU Delegation guidance.

Create a dedicated inter-institutional working group linking the SPO, Ministry of Justice, and Ministry of EU Integration to systematically document accountability outcomes and feed them into Chapter 23/24 final benchmark reporting.

Pursue parallel civil recovery proceedings against former officials for documented financial damages to the state (the EUR 280,000+ in compensation paid), separate from criminal proceedings, to demonstrate comprehensive rule-of-law response.
Allocate IPA III technical assistance funds specifically to strengthen SPO capacity for complex high-level cases, including secondment of EU prosecutors as advisors.
expected_benefits: Stronger compliance with ECHR proportionality standards, reducing risk of procedural criticism that could undermine the prosecution's credibility with Brussels.
Formal non-interference protocols would provide documented evidence for Chapter 23 final benchmark assessment that Montenegro protects prosecutorial autonomy.
Systematic documentation of accountability outcomes would create a structured evidence base for the European Commission's assessment of Montenegro's anti-corruption track record.
Civil recovery proceedings would demonstrate comprehensive state response and potentially recoup the EUR 280,000+ in damages, addressing both accountability and fiscal responsibility.
Enhanced SPO capacity through IPA-funded EU technical assistance would directly address the Commission's concern about insufficient human resources and infrastructure.
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: High feasibility. The criminal prosecution is already underway and need not be altered -- the counter-proposal adds complementary mechanisms. The non-interference protocol requires only a Government decision. Civil recovery proceedings can be initiated by the State Attorney. IPA III technical assistance reallocation requires coordination with the EU Delegation but falls within existing programming frameworks. The inter-institutional working group can be established by ministerial decision. All measures are implementable within the Q3 2026 deadline for Chapter 23/24 final benchmarks.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Okvir odgovornosti usklađen s EU za visoke zloupotrebe funkcije
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Umjesto da se oslanja isključivo na krivično gonjenje s postupcima hapšenja koji izazivaju kritike u pogledu proporcionalnosti, Ministarstvo evropskih integracija zalagalo bi se za sveobuhvatni okvir odgovornosti koji kombinuje krivično gonjenje s administrativnim i građanskim mehanizmima povrata imovine, transparentnu komunikaciju i izričit politički konsenzus o nemiješanju — sve strukturirano kako bi se maksimizovala pozitivna ocjena u okviru završnih mjerila Poglavlja 23 i 24.
key_changes_mne: Osigurati proporcionalnu primjenu prinudnih mjera: poziv umjesto hapšenja u slučajevima nenasilne zloupotrebe funkcije gdje ne postoji rizik od bjekstva, uz zadržavanje hapšenja za slučajeve u kojima je ono dokazivo neophodno prema jurisprudenciji Evropskog suda za ljudska prava.

Uspostaviti formalni vladini protokol kojim se od svih političkih funkcionera (uključujući predsjednika Skupštine) zahtijeva izdavanje standardizovanih izjava o nemiješanju kada tužilačke radnje ciljaju bivše zvaničnike, usklađeno sa smjernicama Delegacije EU.

Osnovati posebnu međuinstitucionalnu radnu grupu koja povezuje Specijalno državno tužilaštvo, Ministarstvo pravde i Ministarstvo evropskih integracija radi sistematskog dokumentovanja ishoda odgovornosti i njihovog uključivanja u izvještavanje o završnim mjerilima Poglavlja 23/24.

Pokrenuti paralelne postupke građanskog povrata imovine protiv bivših zvaničnika za dokumentovanu finansijsku štetu nanesenu državi (više od 280.000 EUR isplaćene naknade), odvojeno od krivičnog postupka, u cilju demonstriranja sveobuhvatnog odgovora vladavine prava.
Dodijeliti sredstva tehničke pomoći IPA III konkretno za jačanje kapaciteta Specijalnog državnog tužilaštva za složene predmete visoke razine, uključujući upućivanje tužilaca EU kao savjetnika.
expected_benefits_mne: Jača usklađenost sa standardima proporcionalnosti EKLJP-a, smanjujući rizik od proceduralnih kritika koje bi mogle narušiti kredibilitet tužilaštva pred Briselom.
Formalni protokoli o nemiješanju pružili bi dokumentovane dokaze za ocjenu završnih mjerila Poglavlja 23 da Crna Gora štiti autonomiju tužilaštva.
Sistematsko dokumentovanje ishoda odgovornosti stvorilo bi strukturisanu bazu dokaza za ocjenu Evropske komisije o antikuruptivnoj evidenciji Crne Gore.
Postupci građanskog povrata imovine demonstrirali bi sveobuhvatan državni odgovor i potencijalno povratili više od 280.000 EUR na ime štete, rješavajući i pitanje odgovornosti i fiskalnu odgovornost.
Ojačani kapaciteti Specijalnog državnog tužilaštva putem tehničke pomoći EU finansirane iz IPA-e direktno bi se bavili zabrinutošću Komisije u pogledu nedovoljnih ljudskih resursa i infrastrukture.
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Visoka izvodljivost. Krivično gonjenje je već u toku i ne mora se mijenjati — kontra-prijedlog dodaje komplementarne mehanizme. Protokol o nemiješanju zahtijeva samo vladinu odluku. Postupke građanskog povrata imovine može pokrenuti Državni pravobranilac. Preraspodjela tehničke pomoći IPA III zahtijeva koordinaciju s Delegacijom EU, ali spada u okvire postojećih programskih okvira. Međuinstitucionalna radna grupa može se osnovati ministarskom odlukom. Sve mjere su sprovodive u okviru roka Q3 2026. za završna mjerila Poglavlja 23/24.

assessments.summary_mne: Hapšenje bivše ministarke prosvjete Vesne Bratić od strane Specijalnog državnog tužilaštva predstavlja značajan korak u izgradnji crnogorskog antikorupcijskog dosjea, što je ključni uslov za zatvaranje Poglavlja 23 i 24 i napredak ka članstvu u EU. Krivično gonjenje bivše ministrice za zloupotrebu položaja -- zasnovano na sudski potvrđenim nezakonitim razrješenjima više od 140 direktora škola i više od 280.000 EUR štete isplaćene iz državnog budžeta -- šalje signal da visoki zvaničnici podliježu odgovornosti. Međutim, politički protivudar opozicionih stranaka i predsjednika Skupštine Mandića, koji je javno kritikovao postupak hapšenja, prijeti narušavanjem tužilačke nezavisnosti, protiv čega je Delegacija EU izričito upozorila. Ukupna ocjena je pozitivna za evropske integracije, pod uslovom da se krivično gonjenje odvija na osnovu merituma predmeta, bez političkog miješanja, i da se zabrinutosti u pogledu proporcionalnosti postupka hapšenja transparentno razriješe.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Ovo hapšenje predstavlja pozitivan signal za crnogorsko pristupanje EU, direktno jačajući antikorupcijski dosje koji se zahtijeva u okviru Poglavlja 23 i 24. Ključna zabrinutost jeste da politička kritika hapšenja od strane predsjednika Skupštine i opozicionih stranaka prijeti narušavanju tužilačke autonomije -- upravo onoga pred čime je Delegacija EU upozorila. Ministarstvo preporučuje da svi politički akteri javno potvrde poštovanje tužilačke nezavisnosti i da SDT obezbijedi potpunu proceduralnu transparentnost radi očuvanja kredibiliteta pred Briselom.
key_concerns_mne: Pog. 23 Pravosuđe i osnovna prava -- Javna kritika hapšenja od strane predsjednika Skupštine Mandića i opozicionih stranaka može biti protumačena u Briselu kao politički pritisak na tužilačku nezavisnost, što je u direktnoj suprotnosti s upozorenjima Delegacije EU da se politički akteri moraju suzdržati od uticaja na tužilačke odluke.

Pog. 23 Pravosuđe i osnovna prava -- Proporcionalnost postupka hapšenja (72-satni pritvor za administrativnu zloupotrebu položaja) može izazvati zabrinutost u pogledu osnovnih prava po standardima EU, što bi potencijalno moglo narušiti signal vladavine prava koji krivično gonjenje treba da pošalje.

Pog. 23 i Pog. 24 -- Etnizacija slučaja, uz narative koji krivično gonjenje prikazuju kao antisrpski progon, prijeti eroziji međustranačkog konsenzusa neophodnog za reforme na putu ka EU i mogla bi zakomplikovati političko okruženje za zatvaranje posljednjih mjerila do trećeg kvartala 2026. godine.

Pog. 23 Pravosuđe i osnovna prava -- Dosjea SDT-a i dalje pokazuje jaz između istraga i pravosnažnih osuđujućih presuda u predmetima visokog profila. Ukoliko ovo krivično gonjenje ne rezultira osuđujućom presudom, moglo bi ojačati percepciju nekažnjivosti na koju je ukazala Evropska komisija.

Pog. 23 i Pog. 24 -- Specijalno državno tužilaštvo i dalje se suočava s infrastrukturnim i kadrovskim ograničenjima navedenim u Izvještaju EU o napretku za 2025. godinu, što postavlja pitanje da li ima kapacitet da istovremeno efikasno vodi više predmeta visokog profila.
recommendations_mne: Svi politički akteri, a posebno predsjednik Skupštine Mandić, trebalo bi da javno potvrde poštovanje tužilačke nezavisnosti u skladu s izričitim smjernicama Delegacije EU. Ministarstvo evropskih integracija trebalo bi da izda saopštenje kojim podsjeća političke aktere da izjave koje se doživljavaju kao pritisak na SDT direktno ugrožavaju napredak u ispunjavanju konačnih mjerila u Poglavlju 23.

SDT bi trebalo proaktivno da komunicira pravni osnov i opravdanje proporcionalnosti postupka hapšenja, transparentno odgovarajući na zabrinutosti javnosti. Time bi se ojačala, a ne oslabila, naracija o vladavini prava u odnosima s Briselom.

Vlada bi trebalo da ubrza sprovođenje Akcionih planova iz aprila 2025. godine za konačna mjerila u Poglavljima 23 i 24, koristeći ovaj slučaj kao dokaz jačanja okvira odgovornosti, uz istovremeno obezbjeđivanje da se predmet vodi striktno na osnovu pravnih merituma.

Specijalnom državnom tužilaštvu trebalo bi dodijeliti dodatna budžetska sredstva i kadrovske resurse kako bi se otklonila ograničenja kapaciteta identifikovana u Izvještaju EU o napretku za 2025. godinu i osiguralo efikasno vođenje više predmeta visokog profila istovremeno.

Ministarstvo evropskih integracija trebalo bi da pripremi brifing za Evropsku komisiju u kojem dokumentuje ovo krivično gonjenje kao dio rastućeg crnogorskog dosjea u oblasti odgovornosti na visokom nivou, uz napomenu o institucionalnim garancijama kojima se osigurava tužilačka nezavisnost od političkog uticaja.

Trebalo bi podstaći međustranački dijalog kako bi se spriječila etnizacija sudskih postupaka, budući da ta dinamika direktno prijeti političkoj stabilnosti i reformskom konsenzusu neophodnom za napredak u procesu pristupanja EU.

assessments.summary: The arrest of former Education Minister Vesna Bratić by the Special State Prosecutor's Office represents a meaningful step in holding high-ranking officials accountable for abuse of office, consistent with Montenegro's EU accession obligations under Chapter 23. Courts have already confirmed that Bratić's mass dismissal of over 140 school principals in 2021 was unlawful, causing documented financial harm to the state exceeding 280,000 euros in compensation payments alone. While the underlying criminal investigation is legally well-founded, the manner of arrest -- involving special police units and physical detention for a non-violent, white-collar offense by a former official with no apparent flight risk -- raises proportionality concerns under both Montenegrin constitutional standards and European Convention on Human Rights (ECHR) case law. The judiciary acted appropriately by subsequently replacing detention with house arrest and electronic monitoring, but the initial heavy-handed approach risks undermining public confidence in impartial justice and fueling legitimate criticism of selective prosecution.
assessments.executive_summary: The criminal investigation into former Minister Bratić is legally justified given court-confirmed unlawful dismissals of over 140 school principals and documented state damages, representing important accountability under EU Chapter 23 standards. However, the disproportionate arrest method -- deploying special police for a non-violent white-collar case -- undermines rule of law credibility and invites legitimate accusations of selective justice. The Ministry of Justice recommends establishing clear proportionality protocols for arrests of public officials in non-violent cases and ensuring consistent prosecution standards to avoid perceptions of political motivation.
assessments.reasoning:
LEGAL FOUNDATION AND MERIT OF THE INVESTIGATION: The criminal investigation into Vesna Bratić rests on solid legal ground. During her tenure as Education Minister in the Krivokapić government (2020-2022), Bratić dismissed over 140 school principals across Montenegro in 2021, citing their failure to form school boards. However, courts systematically found these dismissals unlawful, determining that the Ministry of Education itself had obstructed board formation by refusing to appoint its own representatives. By early 2026, approximately 157 court proceedings had been initiated by dismissed directors, with over 50 first-instance verdicts ruling in their favour (as reported by CdM and confirmed by the Defender of Property and Legal Interests of Montenegro). The state has been obligated to pay compensation exceeding 280,000 euros, with potential total damages described by the Special State Prosecutor's Office as reaching into the millions. Under Article 416 of the Montenegrin Criminal Code, abuse of official position that causes material damage to public funds constitutes a criminal offense. The systematic nature of Bratić's actions -- identical dismissal letters sent to over 140 principals, in circumstances where the Ministry itself was the obstructing party -- goes beyond a mere administrative error and enters the territory of deliberate misuse of ministerial authority. This makes criminal investigation appropriate and proportionate to the gravity of the alleged conduct.

EU ACCESSION CONTEXT AND CHAPTER 23 IMPLICATIONS: Montenegro's EU accession process places particular emphasis on Chapter 23 (Judiciary and Fundamental Rights), which requires demonstrable progress in prosecuting high-level corruption and abuse of office. The European Commission's 2025 Rule of Law Report and Montenegro's own action plans for final benchmarks in Chapters 23 and 24 explicitly require increased investigations and convictions in corruption-related cases. The prosecution of a former minister sends an important signal that no official is above the law, which is a core requirement under the EU acquis. This case also follows other notable prosecutions of high-ranking officials, including the conviction of the former Supreme Court President in 2024. However, the EU also requires that such prosecutions meet standards of proportionality, due process, and non-selectivity -- areas where this case raises concerns.

PROPORTIONALITY AND ARREST PROCEDURE CONCERNS: The manner of Bratić's arrest is the most problematic aspect of this case from a justice perspective. Deploying special police units to arrest a former minister on suspicion of a non-violent, white-collar offense raises serious questions about proportionality under Article 5 of the ECHR and Article 29 of the Montenegrin Constitution (right to liberty). The Criminal Procedure Code of Montenegro permits detention only when specific conditions are met: flight risk, evidence tampering risk, or risk of reoffending. For a former minister no longer in office, none of these conditions appear obviously met. The judiciary itself implicitly acknowledged this disproportionality by replacing detention with house arrest under electronic monitoring and a 50-metre no-contact order with witnesses after her hearing. This suggests that less restrictive measures were available from the outset and should have been applied initially. The NSD's criticism that Bratić was "led away like a hardened criminal while the top of the criminal octopus still walks the streets of Montenegro freely" -- while politically motivated -- touches on a legitimate legal principle: proportionality in the application of coercive measures.

SELECTIVE PROSECUTION RISK: A critical concern for the Ministry of Justice is whether this prosecution is applied consistently. If former officials from certain political orientations face aggressive prosecution while others accused of comparable or more serious offenses remain unprosecuted, this would constitute selective justice -- a violation of the equal protection clause under Article 8 of the Montenegrin Constitution and contrary to EU rule of law standards. The political context matters: Bratić is associated with the pro-Serbian conservative political alignment, and the simultaneous arrest operations targeting Blažo Šaranović (former property management director from the same government) could suggest a pattern. The Ministry of Justice must insist that prosecution decisions are based solely on evidence and legal merit, not political alignment. The EU's 2025 Rule of Law Report for Montenegro already notes concerns about prosecutorial accountability, with mechanisms for integrity of prosecutors described as "not efficient" and instances where prosecutors who made mistakes in prominent cases were promoted rather than disciplined.

JUDICIAL INDEPENDENCE AND COURT FUNCTIONING: Positively, the fact that courts consistently ruled in favour of the dismissed principals demonstrates the functioning of judicial independence in Montenegro. The administrative courts operated without apparent political interference, reaching decisions contrary to the policies of the sitting government. This is an encouraging indicator for Chapter 23 compliance. However, the case also reveals systemic weaknesses: 157 separate court proceedings arising from one minister's actions represent an enormous burden on an already strained judicial system, and the compensation payments represent a direct cost to taxpayers caused by executive overreach.

HUMAN RIGHTS CONSIDERATIONS: The 72-hour detention period, while within the legal maximum, must be scrutinized for ECHR compliance. Article 5(3) of the ECHR requires that detained persons be brought promptly before a judge. The court's subsequent decision to impose house arrest with electronic monitoring rather than continued detention is consistent with ECHR case law favouring the least restrictive measure, but the initial arrest procedure itself may be vulnerable to challenge before the European Court of Human Rights as disproportionate to the nature of the alleged offense.

CONSTITUTIONAL COMPLIANCE: The investigation and prosecution of a former minister is constitutionally permissible under Montenegrin law -- ministers do not enjoy immunity from criminal prosecution after leaving office. The Special State Prosecutor's Office has jurisdiction over cases involving organized crime, corruption, and abuse of office by high officials. The procedural framework is therefore constitutionally sound, even if its execution raises proportionality questions.
key_concerns: The arrest procedure -- deploying special police for a non-violent white-collar offense by a former official -- was disproportionate under ECHR Article 5 and Montenegrin constitutional standards, as the court itself implicitly acknowledged by replacing detention with house arrest and electronic monitoring.

Risk of selective prosecution: the case must be evaluated against whether officials from all political backgrounds face equal scrutiny for comparable conduct, not just those associated with the Krivokapić government.

The Special State Prosecutor's Office opened this case in early 2023 but the arrest came only in February 2026 -- a three-year gap that raises questions about whether the timing was influenced by political considerations rather than purely prosecutorial logic.

The systematic nature of the unlawful dismissals (over 140 identical decisions) caused significant institutional damage to the education system and financial harm to the state, yet the prosecution scope may be too narrowly focused on Bratić individually rather than addressing the systemic decision-making failures.

Parliamentary Speaker Mandić's public criticism of the arrest procedure, while potentially politically motivated, risks being perceived as executive interference with prosecutorial independence -- a concern under Chapter 23 benchmarks.

The case creates precedent that could either strengthen or weaken accountability depending on its outcome: acquittal on procedural grounds would undermine future anti-corruption efforts, while conviction based on a disproportionate process could be overturned at the ECHR level.
recommendations: Establish binding prosecutorial guidelines on proportionality of coercive measures for non-violent offenses, particularly for former public officials where flight risk and evidence tampering risk are minimal -- summoning for questioning should be the default, not arrest by special police units.

Conduct a comprehensive review of all pending abuse-of-office cases involving former ministers and senior officials across all governments to ensure consistent, non-selective application of criminal law regardless of political affiliation.

Expand the investigation beyond individual criminal liability to examine systemic failures in ministerial oversight mechanisms that allowed one minister to issue over 140 unlawful dismissal decisions without internal legal review or intervention.

Strengthen pre-arrest judicial review by requiring prosecutors to demonstrate to a judge that less restrictive measures are insufficient before authorizing arrest in non-violent cases, consistent with ECHR Article 5(3) standards.

Accelerate the timeline for high-profile abuse-of-office prosecutions: the three-year gap between case opening (2023) and arrest (2026) undermines both deterrence and public confidence in the justice system.

Use this case as an opportunity to develop a comprehensive legal framework for ministerial accountability that clearly distinguishes between poor policy decisions (which should face political, not criminal, consequences) and deliberate abuse of authority (which warrants criminal prosecution).

Ensure full transparency in the prosecution by publishing redacted case summaries and procedural decisions, consistent with EU best practices on open justice and the European Commission's rule of law recommendations for Montenegro.

counter_proposal.title: Proportionate Accountability Framework for Ministerial Abuse of Office
counter_proposal.summary: Rather than relying on dramatic arrest procedures that invite accusations of political theatre, the Ministry of Justice would implement a structured accountability protocol that distinguishes between the severity of misconduct, applies proportionate procedural measures, and ensures consistent prosecution across all political affiliations. The approach would involve summoning former officials for questioning as the default procedure, reserving arrest for cases involving flight risk or evidence destruction, while simultaneously broadening the investigation to include systemic institutional failures that enabled the unlawful dismissals.
key_changes: Replace arrest-first approach with summons-first protocol for non-violent abuse-of-office cases involving former officials no longer in positions of power.

Establish a cross-governmental audit of all ministerial decisions that resulted in court-ordered compensation to the state, identifying patterns of systemic abuse regardless of which government was responsible.

Create a dedicated ministerial accountability unit within the Special State Prosecutor's Office with clear mandate to investigate abuse of office by current and former officials of all political backgrounds, with published annual statistics on cases by political affiliation.

Implement mandatory internal legal review for ministerial decisions affecting more than 10 individuals simultaneously, creating an institutional safeguard against mass unlawful actions like the school principal dismissals.
expected_benefits: Eliminates perception of selective or politically motivated prosecution by applying consistent procedural standards across all cases.
Reduces ECHR vulnerability by ensuring proportionality of coercive measures from the outset rather than correcting course after the fact.
Strengthens Montenegro's Chapter 23 compliance by demonstrating mature, institutionalized accountability mechanisms rather than high-profile but procedurally questionable arrests.
Creates systemic prevention of mass unlawful ministerial decisions through mandatory legal review, reducing future state liability.
Builds public confidence in prosecutorial independence by publishing transparent, disaggregated statistics on prosecution patterns.
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: High feasibility. Most proposed changes involve procedural guidelines and institutional protocols that can be implemented through prosecutorial directives and amendments to the Rules of Procedure of the Special State Prosecutor's Office, without requiring legislative changes. The mandatory legal review mechanism for mass ministerial decisions would require a minor amendment to the Law on State Administration, achievable within a single parliamentary cycle. The cross-governmental audit is resource-intensive but could be phased over 12-18 months using existing judicial and prosecutorial staff.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Okvir srazmjerne odgovornosti za zloupotrebu položaja od strane ministara
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Umjesto oslanjanja na dramatične postupke hapšenja koji izazivaju optužbe za političku predstavu, Ministarstvo pravde bi uvelo strukturisani protokol odgovornosti koji pravi razliku između stepena težine prekršaja, primjenjuje srazmjerne proceduralne mjere i osigurava dosljednost krivičnog gonjenja bez obzira na političku pripadnost. Pristup bi podrazumijevao pozivanje bivših zvaničnika na saslušanje kao podrazumijevanu proceduru, uz zadržavanje hapšenja za slučajeve koji uključuju rizik od bijega ili uništavanja dokaza, dok bi se istovremeno proširila istraga na sistemske institucionalne propuste koji su omogućili nezakonita razrješenja.
key_changes_mne: Zamijeniti pristup zasnovan na prvom hapšenju protokolom zasnovanim na prvom pozivu za nenasilne slučajeve zloupotrebe položaja koji se tiču bivših zvaničnika koji više nisu na vlasti.

Uspostaviti međuvladinu reviziju svih ministarskih odluka koje su rezultirale sudski naloženom naknadom štete na teret države, uz identifikovanje obrazaca sistemske zloupotrebe bez obzira na to koja je vlada bila odgovorna.

Osnovati posebnu jedinicu za ministarsku odgovornost u okviru Specijalnog državnog tužilaštva s jasnim mandatom za istragu zloupotrebe položaja od strane sadašnjih i bivših zvaničnika svih političkih profila, uz objavljivanje godišnjih statistika o predmetima po političkoj pripadnosti.

Uvesti obaveznu internu pravnu provjeru ministarskih odluka koje istovremeno utiču na više od 10 lica, čime se stvara institucionalna zaštita od masovnih nezakonitih radnji poput razrješenja direktora škola.
expected_benefits_mne: Eliminiše percepciju selektivnog ili politički motivisanog krivičnog gonjenja primjenom dosljednih proceduralnih standarda u svim predmetima.
Smanjuje ranjivost pred EKLJP-om osiguravanjem srazmjernosti prinudnih mjera od samog početka, umjesto naknadnog ispravljanja kursa.
Jača usklađenost Crne Gore s Poglavljem 23 demonstriranjem zrelih, institucionalizovanih mehanizama odgovornosti, umjesto visokoprofilnih, ali proceduralno upitnih hapšenja.
Stvara sistemsku prevenciju masovnih nezakonitih ministarskih odluka kroz obaveznu pravnu provjeru, čime se smanjuje buduća odgovornost države.
Gradi povjerenje javnosti u nezavisnost tužilaštva objavljivanjem transparentnih, disagregiranih statistika o obrascima krivičnog gonjenja.
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Visoka izvodljivost. Većina predloženih izmjena odnosi se na proceduralne smjernice i institucionalne protokole koji se mogu sprovesti putem tužilačkih direktiva i izmjena Poslovnika o radu Specijalnog državnog tužilaštva, bez potrebe za zakonodavnim izmjenama. Mehanizam obavezne pravne provjere za masovne ministarske odluke zahtijevao bi manju izmjenu Zakona o državnoj upravi, što je ostvarivo u okviru jednog parlamentarnog ciklusa. Međuvladina revizija zahtijeva značajne resurse, ali bi se mogla odvijati fazno u periodu od 12 do 18 mjeseci uz korišćenje postojećeg sudskog i tužilačkog osoblja.

assessments.summary_mne: Hapšenje bivše ministarke prosvjete Vesne Bratić od strane Specijalnog državnog tužilaštva predstavlja značajan korak ka odgovornosti visokih zvaničnika za zloupotrebu službenog položaja, u skladu s obavezama Crne Gore u okviru Poglavlja 23 u procesu pristupanja EU. Sudovi su već potvrdili da je masovno razrješenje više od 140 direktora škola koje je Bratić sprovela 2021. godine bilo nezakonito, prouzrokujući dokumentovanu finansijsku štetu državi koja samo po osnovu isplata odšteta premašuje 280.000 eura. Iako je sama krivična istraga pravno dobro utemeljena, način hapšenja -- uz angažovanje specijalnih policijskih jedinica i fizičko lišavanje slobode zbog nenasilnog, krivičnog djela bijelog okovratnika od strane bivšeg zvaničnika kod kojeg ne postoji očigledan rizik od bjekstva -- izaziva zabrinutost u pogledu proporcionalnosti, kako po crnogorskim ustavnim standardima tako i po sudskoj praksi Evropske konvencije o ljudskim pravima (EKLJP).
