Pravosuđe je postupilo primjereno time što je naknadno zamijenilo pritvor kućnim pritvором i elektronskim nadzorom, ali je početni pretjerano rigidan pristup mogao narušiti povjerenje javnosti u nepristrasno pravosuđe i dati povoda opravdanoj kritici o selektivnom gonjenju.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Krivična istraga protiv bivše ministarke Bratić pravno je opravdana s obzirom na sudski potvrđena nezakonita razrješenja više od 140 direktora škola i dokumentovanu štetu za državu, što predstavlja važan korak u odgovornosti u skladu sa standardima Poglavlja 23 EU. Međutim, nesrazmjeran način hapšenja -- angažovanje specijalne policije u nenasilnom predmetu bijelog okovratnika -- narušava kredibilitet vladavine prava i poziva opravdane optužbe za selektivno pravosuđe. Ministarstvo pravde preporučuje uspostavljanje jasnih protokola proporcionalnosti za hapšenje javnih zvaničnika u nenasilnim slučajevima i osiguravanje dosljednih standarda gonjenja kako bi se izbjegla percepcija političke motivacije.
key_concerns_mne: Postupak hapšenja -- angažovanje specijalne policije zbog nenasilnog krivičnog djela bijelog okovratnika od strane bivšeg zvaničnika -- bio je nesrazmjeran u skladu s članom 5 EKLJP-a i crnogorskim ustavnim standardima, što je i sam sud implicitno priznao zamjenom pritvora kućnim pritvором i elektronskim nadzorom.

Rizik od selektivnog gonjenja: slučaj se mora ocjenjivati u kontekstu toga da li zvaničnici iz svih političkih sredina podliježu jednakom nadzoru zbog uporedivih postupaka, a ne samo oni povezani s Krivokapićevom vladom.

Specijalno državno tužilaštvo otvorilo je ovaj predmet početkom 2023. godine, a hapšenje je uslijedilo tek u februaru 2026. -- trogodišnji razmak koji otvara pitanje da li je tajming bio pod uticajem političkih razmatranja, a ne isključivo tužilačke logike.

Sistemski karakter nezakonitih razrješenja (više od 140 identičnih odluka) nanio je značajnu institucionalnu štetu obrazovnom sistemu i finansijsku štetu državi, a ipak se čini da je opseg gonjenja previše usko usmjeren na Bratić kao pojedinca, umjesto da se bavi sistemskim propustima u donošenju odluka.

Javna kritika predsjednika Skupštine Mandića na račun postupka hapšenja, iako potencijalno politički motivisana, mogла bi biti protumačena kao izvršno miješanje u tužilačku nezavisnost -- što predstavlja zabrinutost u kontekstu mjerila Poglavlja 23.

Slučaj stvara presedan koji, ovisno o ishodu, može ili ojačati ili oslabiti odgovornost: oslobađajuća presuda iz procesnih razloga narušila bi buduće antikorupcijske napore, dok bi osuđujuća presuda zasnovana na nesrazmjernom postupku mogla biti ukinuta na nivou EKLJP-a.
recommendations_mne: Uspostaviti obavezujuće tužilačke smjernice o proporcionalnosti prinudnih mjera za nenasilna krivična djela, posebno kada su u pitanju bivši javni zvaničnici kod kojih su rizik od bjekstva i rizik od uticaja na dokaze minimalni -- pozivanje na saslušanje treba biti podrazumijevana mjera, a ne hapšenje uz angažovanje specijalnih policijskih jedinica.

Sprovesti sveobuhvatnu reviziju svih predmeta zloupotrebe službenog položaja u toku koji se odnose na bivše ministre i visoke zvaničnike svih vlada, kako bi se osigurala dosljedna i neselektivna primjena krivičnog prava bez obzira na političku pripadnost.

Proširiti istragu van individualne krivične odgovornosti kako bi se ispitali sistemski propusti u mehanizmima ministarskog nadzora, koji su jednoj ministarki omogućili da donese više od 140 nezakonitih odluka o razrješenju bez internog pravnog preispitivanja ili intervencije.

Ojačati sudsko preispitivanje prije hapšenja uvođenjem obaveze da tužioci pred sudom dokažu da su restriktivnije mjere nedovoljne prije nego što se odobri hapšenje u nenasilnim slučajevima, u skladu sa standardima iz člana 5(3) EKLJP-a.

Ubrzati dinamiku gonjenja u visokoprofilnim predmetima zloupotrebe službenog položaja: trogodišnji razmak između otvaranja predmeta (2023) i hapšenja (2026) slabi i odvraćajući efekat i povjerenje javnosti u pravosudni sistem.

Iskoristiti ovaj slučaj kao priliku za razvijanje sveobuhvatnog pravnog okvira za ministarsku odgovornost koji jasno razgraničava loše odluke u oblasti politike (koje bi trebalo da nose političke, a ne krivične posljedice) od namjerne zloupotrebe ovlašćenja (koja opravdava krivično gonjenje).

Osigurati punu transparentnost gonjenja objavljivanjem redaktovanih sažetaka predmeta i procesnih odluka, u skladu s najboljom praksom EU o otvorenom pravosuđu i preporukama Evropske komisije o vladavini prava za Crnu Goru.

debate.decision_id: Former Education Minister Vesna Bratić Arrested for Abuse of Office
debate.consensus_summary: All nine ministries agree on several fundamental points. First, the mass dismissal of over 140 school principals in 2021 was unlawful, as confirmed by courts in over 130 proceedings, and caused documented harm to both the education system and the state budget (exceeding EUR 280,000 in compensation, with the final cost likely much higher). Second, holding former officials accountable for abuse of office is consistent with Montenegro's EU accession obligations under Chapters 23 and 24, and sends a necessary signal against impunity. Third, the manner of the arrest -- deployment of special police units, handcuffs, and 72-hour detention for a non-violent, white-collar offense by a former official with no apparent flight risk -- was disproportionate and risks undermining the legitimate rule-of-law signal the prosecution is meant to send. Fourth, systemic reforms are urgently needed to prevent future politically motivated mass dismissals in the public sector, including transferring school director appointment authority away from the minister and establishing mandatory legal review and fiscal impact assessments before large-scale personnel decisions. Fifth, the political polarization and ethnicization of the case threaten to erode the cross-party consensus needed for EU accession and to damage Montenegro's governance credibility with international partners and investors. Finally, the three-year gap between the CGO criminal complaint in November 2022 and the arrest in February 2026 raises legitimate questions about prosecutorial timeliness that must be addressed transparently.
disagreements: Whether the prosecution represents genuine, impartial accountability or selective, politically motivated justice -- the Ministry of Justice and EU Integration view it as a meaningful accountability step, while the Ministry of Interior highlights the perception of selective enforcement that undermines institutional credibility.

The proportionality of the arrest procedure: the Ministry of Interior and Ministry of Justice both flag the use of special police, handcuffs, and 72-hour detention as excessive for a non-violent administrative offense, while the Ministry of EU Integration and Finance focus more on the positive rule-of-law signal, noting that proportionality concerns can be addressed without discrediting the prosecution itself.

The net impact on EU accession: the Ministry of EU Integration rates this most positively (7/10) as building the anti-corruption track record Brussels demands, while the Ministry of Tourism warns that the politicized backlash and perception of selective justice may actually undermine EU confidence in Montenegro's judiciary reforms.

Whether the prosecution scope is adequate: the Ministry of Justice argues the investigation may be too narrowly focused on Bratic individually rather than addressing the systemic decision-making failures and institutional mechanisms that enabled 140+ identical unlawful decisions, while the Ministry of Education calls for the case to catalyze broader governance reform in the education sector.

The fiscal significance of the case: the Ministry of Finance views the EUR 280,000+ in compensation as part of a broader EUR 4 million pattern of governance-failure costs since 2020 warranting systemic fiscal safeguards, while the Ministry of Health and Environment view the fiscal impact as relatively minor but symptomatic of budget misallocation away from their sectors.

The timing and political context of the arrest: several ministries note the three-year delay between the criminal complaint and the arrest raises questions about whether prosecutorial timing was influenced by political calculations rather than evidentiary requirements, which could undermine the deterrent effect and public trust in impartial justice.
debate.debate_transcript:
PARLIAMENTARY DEBATE ON THE ARREST OF FORMER EDUCATION MINISTER VESNA BRATIC FOR ABUSE OF OFFICE -- Skupstina Crne Gore, 22 February 2026

SESSION OPENING

The session opened with the presiding officer noting that nine government ministries had submitted assessments of the arrest of former Education Minister Vesna Bratic by the Special State Prosecutor's Office on 16 February 2026. The ministries collectively assigned verdicts ranging from neutral (5/10) to positive (7/10), with an aggregate assessment that the prosecution is a modestly positive development, heavily conditioned on procedural fairness, political non-interference, and systemic reform.

PRESENTATION OF MINISTRY POSITIONS

The Ministry of Justice (verdict: positive, 7/10) presented the case as a meaningful step toward high-level accountability. Courts have already confirmed that Bratic's mass dismissal of over 140 school principals was unlawful, generating over EUR 280,000 in state-paid damages. The Ministry emphasized, however, that the arrest procedure was disproportionate under both Montenegrin constitutional standards and European Convention on Human Rights Article 5, noting the court itself implicitly acknowledged this by replacing detention with house arrest and electronic monitoring. The Ministry called for binding prosecutorial guidelines on proportionality for non-violent offenses and a comprehensive review of all pending abuse-of-office cases across all governments to ensure non-selective application.

The Ministry of EU Integration (verdict: positive, 7/10) framed the prosecution as critical for building Montenegro's anti-corruption track record under Chapters 23 and 24. It warned, however, that public criticism of the arrest by Parliament Speaker Mandic risks being interpreted by Brussels as political pressure on prosecutorial independence, directly contradicting EU Delegation guidance that political actors must refrain from influencing prosecutorial decisions. The Ministry stressed that Montenegro's ambition to close all negotiation chapters by end of 2026 requires demonstrating structural reform capacity, not just individual prosecutions.

The Ministry of Finance (verdict: positive, 6/10) focused on fiscal accountability, noting the EUR 280,000 in documented compensation is part of a broader EUR 4 million pattern of governance-failure costs since 2020. The Ministry called for mandatory fiscal impact assessments before mass administrative actions and civil recovery proceedings against responsible officials.

The Ministry of Education (verdict: positive, 6/10) described Bratic's actions as one of the most disruptive episodes in recent Montenegrin education history. The Ministry highlighted that the systemic vulnerability remains: the minister still holds legal authority to elect and dismiss school directors, meaning a future minister could repeat this pattern. The three-year delay between the CGO criminal complaint and the arrest was flagged as undermining deterrent effect.

The Ministry of Interior (verdict: neutral, 5/10) offered the most detailed critique of the arrest procedure, noting that deployment of special police units and handcuffs for a non-violent administrative offense exceeded what was warranted. The Ministry flagged the absence of codified proportionality guidelines for police arrest procedures in Montenegro and warned that inconsistent application of coercive measures fuels perceptions of selective enforcement.

The Ministries of Health, Economy and Labour, Tourism, and Environment (all neutral, 5/10) acknowledged the case falls outside their core mandates but identified indirect impacts: fiscal waste diverting resources from health and social protection, disruption to school-based health services during the post-COVID period, harm to human capital formation exacerbating youth unemployment and emigration, and negative effects on investor confidence and the tourism investment climate.

PARTY-BLOC DEBATE

The DF/DNP bloc (Democratic Front / Democratic People's Party) opened the opposition interventions with forceful condemnation. The bloc characterized the arrest as a political persecution of a minister whose only offense was having the courage to dismantle the deeply entrenched DPS apparatus in the education system. The DNP stated that if dismantling an institutional network built over thirty years of one-party rule constitutes a crime, then all citizens who voted for change on 30 August 2020 should also be arrested. The bloc drew attention to the dramatic manner of the arrest, arguing that presenting a university professor in handcuffs as if she were a dangerous criminal represents a deliberate spectacle designed to humiliate and intimidate. Parliament Speaker Mandic, aligned with this bloc, had publicly stated that clan members, loan sharks, and criminals are smiling at the photographs of the arrested professor, and warned that those who carelessly put handcuffs on others will themselves face the same treatment. The DF/DNP bloc also raised the ethnic dimension, noting that allies in the Serbian parliament had characterized the prosecution as persecution of a person for being Serbian and a believer of the Serbian Orthodox Church. The bloc demanded the immediate release of Bratic and called for the Special State Prosecutor to answer questions about the selective application of the law.

The DPS-led opposition (Democratic Party of Socialists and Social Democratic Party) adopted a notably different posture from the DF/DNP bloc. As the party whose appointees were among those dismissed by Bratic in 2021, the DPS viewed the prosecution as long-overdue validation that the mass dismissals were unlawful. However, the DPS also criticized the arrest procedure as heavy-handed and questioned the timing, asking why the Special State Prosecutor's Office needed three years from the CGO complaint to act. The DPS-SDP bloc argued that the ruling coalition is attempting to instrumentalize the prosecution to project a rule-of-law image to Brussels while simultaneously tolerating political interference from figures like Speaker Mandic who openly pressure the judiciary. The opposition bloc pointed to the broader pattern: if the government is serious about accountability, it should explain why comparable or more serious abuse-of-office cases from other governments have not received similar prosecutorial attention. The DPS contended that genuine anti-corruption progress requires consistent, non-selective application of the law across all political affiliations, not theatrical arrests that conveniently target only political opponents of the current constellation.

The ruling Europe Now!/URA coalition defended the institutional framework while carefully distancing itself from the specifics of the arrest procedure. The coalition argued that the prosecution is an independent action of the Special State Prosecutor's Office, not a government decision, and that political actors from all sides must respect prosecutorial independence as the EU Delegation has explicitly demanded. The coalition pointed to the Ministry assessments, particularly from EU Integration and Justice, which confirm that the prosecution advances Montenegro's Chapter 23 track record. On the proportionality question, the coalition acknowledged that arrest procedures should be calibrated to the nature of the offense and the risk profile of the suspect, and expressed support for the Ministry of Interior's recommendation to develop formal operational guidelines. The coalition emphasized that Montenegro's EU accession timeline demands that all political actors focus on structural reform rather than turning every judicial proceeding into a partisan battlefield. The Europe Now! bloc cited the Ministry of Finance assessment showing EUR 4 million in governance-failure costs since 2020 and argued this pattern of fiscal damage from politicized personnel decisions must end through systemic safeguards, not through partisan finger-pointing.

The Bosnjacka stranka and Albanian minority parties intervened to redirect the debate toward the institutional and human-rights dimensions. These parties noted that the mass dismissal of 140 school principals in 2021 was not merely a political dispute but an act that disrupted educational services for thousands of children, disproportionately affecting minority communities in mixed-population municipalities. The minority bloc supported the principle of accountability and echoed the Ministry of Education's recommendation to transfer director appointment authority to school boards with parent, teacher, and municipal representation, which would better protect minority representation in school governance. On the ethnic framing promoted by the DF/DNP bloc, the minority parties rejected the narrative that Bratic is being prosecuted for her ethnicity, noting that such framing instrumentalizes identity politics to shield officials from legitimate legal accountability. The minority bloc cited the CdM analysis that Bratic is not on trial because she is Serbian, and noted that the leadership of the Special State Prosecutor's Office itself undermines claims of anti-Serbian bias. These parties called for the debate to center on institutional reform and children's educational rights rather than ethnic mobilization.

POINTS OF CONVERGENCE

Despite sharp disagreements on the political interpretation of the arrest, several points of cross-party convergence emerged during the debate.

First, no party bloc defended the substance of Bratic's 2021 actions. Even the DF/DNP bloc, which characterized the dismantling of the DPS apparatus as necessary, did not argue that the specific legal procedure used for the dismissals was correct. The court findings of unlawfulness were not disputed by any parliamentary faction.

Second, broad agreement existed that the arrest procedure was disproportionate. This criticism came from all sides of the political spectrum, from the DF/DNP bloc which called it humiliating, from the DPS which called it heavy-handed, and from the ruling coalition which acknowledged procedural concerns. The Ministry of Interior's recommendation for formal proportionality guidelines received cross-party endorsement.

Third, all blocs expressed support, in principle, for systemic reform to prevent future politically motivated mass dismissals. The Ministry of Education's recommendation to transfer director appointment authority from ministers to school boards was referenced approvingly across the political spectrum, though parties differed on implementation details.

Fourth, there was agreement that the three-year delay between complaint and arrest is problematic and that the Special State Prosecutor's Office must explain the timeline transparently.

POINTS OF CONTENTION

The principal disagreement centered on whether the prosecution is impartial or selective. The DF/DNP bloc insists it is ethnically and politically motivated persecution. The DPS opposition questions whether it is genuinely non-selective given the absence of comparable prosecutions against officials from other governments. The ruling coalition maintains it is an independent prosecutorial decision that should be judged on its legal merits.

The ethnicization of the case represents a deep fault line. The DF/DNP bloc and allied voices from Belgrade frame the prosecution as anti-Serbian persecution, while minority parties and the ruling coalition reject this framing as instrumentalizing identity to obstruct accountability. The Ministry of EU Integration warned that this dynamic directly threatens the political stability and reform consensus required for EU accession.

The role of Parliament Speaker Mandic emerged as a contentious issue. The Ministry of EU Integration explicitly warned that Mandic's public criticism of the arrest risks being interpreted by Brussels as political pressure on prosecutorial independence. The DPS opposition demanded accountability for what it described as the Speaker of Parliament openly interfering with judicial proceedings. The DF/DNP bloc defended Mandic's statements as legitimate political commentary and freedom of expression.

WEIGHING THE NATIONAL INTEREST

The Skupstina notes that Montenegro faces a pivotal moment in its EU accession process, with the ambition to close all negotiation chapters by end of 2026. In this context, the prosecution of a former minister for documented abuse of office serves Montenegro's strategic interest in building the credible anti-corruption track record that Brussels demands. However, this benefit is conditional on the prosecution proceeding on legal merit, free from political interference, and with procedural standards that withstand European Court of Human Rights scrutiny.

The fiscal dimension is significant but secondary. The EUR 280,000 in documented compensation, likely to grow as remaining cases are adjudicated, represents avoidable waste that could have funded educational improvements. The Ministry of Finance's identification of a broader EUR 4 million pattern of governance-failure costs since 2020 underscores the need for systemic fiscal safeguards.

The institutional damage to the education system is the most lasting consequence. Over 140 schools experienced leadership disruption during the critical post-COVID recovery period. The Ministry of Education's assessment that the systemic vulnerability remains, because the minister still holds authority to dismiss directors, means that without legislative reform this episode could be repeated by any future government.

The political polarization triggered by the arrest poses a genuine risk to Montenegro's governance stability and EU accession progress. The ethnicization of what is fundamentally a rule-of-law case threatens to deepen societal divisions and provides external actors with leverage to obstruct Montenegro's Euro-Atlantic integration.

AGGREGATE ASSESSMENT

The Skupstina finds that the prosecution of former Minister Bratic is, on balance, a positive development for accountability and rule of law in Montenegro. Four ministries rated the decision positively (Finance, Education, EU Integration, Justice) and five rated it neutral (Environment, Tourism, Economy, Health, Interior), with no ministry rating it negatively. The aggregate verdict is positive, reflecting the following balance: the underlying conduct was clearly unlawful and caused documented harm; accountability for former ministers is essential for EU accession and governance credibility; however, the proportionality of the arrest procedure must be addressed, the prosecution must demonstrate non-selectivity by applying equal standards to officials of all political backgrounds, and systemic reforms must be enacted to prevent recurrence. The Skupstina calls on all political actors to respect prosecutorial independence, refrain from ethnicizing judicial proceedings, and prioritize the legislative reforms recommended by the Ministries of Education, Justice, Interior, and Finance to ensure that this episode results in lasting institutional improvement rather than merely another chapter in Montenegro's cycle of political retribution.
debate.consensus_summary_mne: Svih devet ministarstava slaže se u nekoliko ključnih tačaka. Prvo, masovno razrješenje više od 140 direktora škola 2021. godine bilo je nezakonito, što su potvrdili sudovi u više od 130 postupaka, i nanijelo je dokumentovanu štetu kako obrazovnom sistemu tako i državnom budžetu (prelazeći 280.000 EUR u naknadama, uz vjerovatno znatno veće konačne troškove). Drugo, pozivanje bivših zvaničnika na odgovornost zbog zloupotrebe službenog položaja u skladu je s obavezama Crne Gore u okviru procesa pristupanja EU prema poglavljima 23 i 24, i šalje neophodan signal protiv nekažnjivosti. Treće, način hapšenja -- angažovanje specijalnih policijskih jedinica, lisice i 72-satni pritvor zbog nenasilnog, kancelarijskog krivičnog djela bivšeg zvaničnika bez očiglednog rizika od bijega -- bio je nesrazmjeran i prijeti narušavanjem legitimnog signala vladavine prava koji bi gonjenje trebalo da pošalje. Četvrto, sistemske reforme hitno su potrebne kako bi se spriječila buduća politički motivisana masovna razrješenja u javnom sektoru, uključujući prenošenje nadležnosti imenovanja direktora škola s ministra i uvođenje obavezne pravne provjere i procjene fiskalnog uticaja prije donošenja odluka o masovnim kadrovskim promjenama. Peto, politička polarizacija i etnicizacija ovog slučaja prijete eroziji međustranačkog konsenzusa neophodnog za pristupanje EU i mogućnosti da se našteti kredibilitetu Crne Gore u oblasti upravljanja pred međunarodnim partnerima i investitorima. Konačno, trogodišnji razmak između krivične prijave CGO-a u novembru 2022. i hapšenja u februaru 2026. pokreće opravdana pitanja o pravodobnosti tužilaštva koja moraju biti transparentno razjašnjena.
disagreements_mne: Da li gonjenje predstavlja istinsku, nepristrasnu odgovornost ili selektivno, politički motivisano pravosuđe -- Ministarstvo pravde i evropskih integracija vidi ga kao smislen korak ka odgovornosti, dok Ministarstvo unutrašnjih poslova ističe percepciju selektivnog sprovođenja zakona koja narušava institucionalnu vjerodostojnost.

Srazmjernost postupka hapšenja: Ministarstvo unutrašnjih poslova i Ministarstvo pravde oba ukazuju da su upotreba specijalne policije, lisica i 72-satni pritvor pretjerani za nenasilno administrativno krivično djelo, dok se Ministarstvo evropskih integracija i Ministarstvo finansija više fokusiraju na pozitivan signal vladavine prava, napominjući da se pitanja srazmjernosti mogu razriješiti bez diskreditovanja samog gonjenja.

Neto uticaj na pristupanje EU: Ministarstvo evropskih integracija ovo ocjenjuje najoptimističnije (7/10) kao izgradnju antikorupcijskog učinka koji Brisel zahtijeva, dok Ministarstvo turizma upozorava da politizovana reakcija i percepcija selektivne pravde mogu zapravo narušiti povjerenje EU u reforme pravosuđa Crne Gore.

Da li je obim gonjenja adekvatan: Ministarstvo pravde smatra da istraga može biti preuzak fokus na Bratić pojedinačno, umjesto da se bavi sistemskim propustima u odlučivanju i institucionalnim mehanizmima koji su omogućili 140+ identičnih nezakonitih odluka, dok Ministarstvo prosvjete poziva da ovaj slučaj podstakne širu reformu upravljanja u obrazovnom sektoru.

Fiskalni značaj slučaja: Ministarstvo finansija smatra da naknada od 280.000+ EUR čini dio šireg obrasca troškova neuspješnog upravljanja u iznosu od 4 miliona EUR od 2020. godine, što zahtijeva sistemske fiskalne zaštitne mehanizme, dok Ministarstvo zdravlja i Ministarstvo ekologije fiskalni uticaj smatraju relativno manjim, ali simptomatičnim za pogrešnu alokaciju budžeta nauštrb njihovih sektora.

Vremenski okvir i politički kontekst hapšenja: nekoliko ministarstava napominje da trogodišnje kašnjenje između krivične prijave i hapšenja pokreće pitanja o tome da li je vremenski okvir tužilaštva bio pod uticajem političkih kalkulacija, a ne dokaznih zahtjeva, što bi moglo narušiti odvraćajući efekat i javno povjerenje u nepristrasno pravosuđe.
debate.debate_transcript_mne:
PARLAMENTARNA DEBATA O HAPŠENJU BIVŠE MINISTARKE PROSVJETE VESNE BRATIĆ ZBOG ZLOUPOTREBE SLUŽBENOG POLOŽAJA -- Skupština Crne Gore, 22. februara 2026.

OTVARANJE SJEDNICE

Sjednica je otvorena uz napomenu predsjedavajućeg da je devet vladinih ministarstava dostavilo ocjene hapšenja bivše ministarke prosvjete Vesne Bratić od strane Specijalnog državnog tužilaštva, 16. februara 2026. Ministarstva su zajednički dodijelila ocjene u rasponu od neutralnih (5/10) do pozitivnih (7/10), s ukupnom ocjenom da je gonjenje skromno pozitivan razvoj događaja, snažno uvjetovan procesnom poštenošću, odsustvom političkog miješanja i sistemskim reformama.

PREZENTACIJA STAVOVA MINISTARSTAVA

Ministarstvo pravde (ocjena: pozitivno, 7/10) predstavilo je slučaj kao smislen korak ka odgovornosti na visokom nivou. Sudovi su već potvrdili da je Bratićevo masovno razrješenje više od 140 direktora škola bilo nezakonito, generišući više od 280.000 EUR naknade štete na teret države. Ministarstvo je ipak naglasilo da je postupak hapšenja bio nesrazmjeran i prema crnogorskim ustavnim standardima i prema članu 5 Evropske konvencije o ljudskim pravima, napominjući da je sud to implicitno priznao zamjenom pritvora kućnim pritvorom i elektronskim nadzorom. Ministarstvo je pozvalo na obavezujuće smjernice tužilaštva o srazmjernosti za nenasilna krivična djela i sveobuhvatni pregled svih slučajeva zloupotrebe službenog položaja koji su u toku u svim vladama, kako bi se osigurala neselektivna primjena.

Ministarstvo evropskih integracija (ocjena: pozitivno, 7/10) predstavilo je gonjenje kao ključno za izgradnju antikorupcijskog učinka Crne Gore u okviru poglavlja 23 i 24. Upozorilo je, međutim, da javna kritika hapšenja od strane predsjednika Skupštine Mandića prijeti da bude protumačena od strane Brisela kao politički pritisak na nezavisnost tužilaštva, čime se direktno proturječi smjernicama Delegacije EU da se politički akteri moraju suzdržati od uticaja na odluke tužilaštva. Ministarstvo je naglasilo da ambicija Crne Gore da zatvori sva poglavlja pregovora do kraja 2026. zahtijeva demonstriranje kapaciteta za strukturne reforme, a ne samo individualna krivična gonjenja.

Ministarstvo finansija (ocjena: pozitivno, 6/10) fokusiralo se na fiskalnu odgovornost, napominjući da 280.000 EUR dokumentovane naknade čini dio šireg obrasca troškova neuspješnog upravljanja od 4 miliona EUR od 2020. godine. Ministarstvo je pozvalo na obavezne procjene fiskalnog uticaja prije masovnih administrativnih mjera i postupke građanskopravne naplate od odgovornih zvaničnika.

Ministarstvo prosvjete (ocjena: pozitivno, 6/10) opisalo je Bratićine postupke kao jednu od najporemećujućih epizoda u novijoj istoriji crnogorskog obrazovanja. Ministarstvo je istaklo da sistemska ranjivost ostaje: ministar i dalje ima zakonsko ovlašćenje za izbor i razrješenje direktora škola, što znači da bi budući ministar mogao ponoviti ovaj obrazac. Trogodišnje kašnjenje između krivične prijave CGO-a i hapšenja označeno je kao faktor koji narušava odvraćajući efekat.

Ministarstvo unutrašnjih poslova (ocjena: neutralno, 5/10) iznijelo je najdetaljniju kritiku postupka hapšenja, napominjući da je angažovanje specijalnih policijskih jedinica i lisica za nenasilno administrativno krivično djelo prevazišlo ono što je bilo opravdano. Ministarstvo je ukazalo na odsustvo kodifikovanih smjernica o srazmjernosti za postupke policijskog hapšenja u Crnoj Gori i upozorilo da nedosljedno primjenjivanje prinudnih mjera hrani percepciju selektivnog sprovođenja zakona.

Ministarstva zdravlja, ekonomije i rada, turizma i ekologije (sva neutralna, 5/10) priznala su da slučaj ne spada u njihov osnovni djelokrug, ali su identifikovala posredne uticaje: fiskalno rasipanje koje preusmjerava sredstva od zdravlja i socijalne zaštite, poremećaj zdravstvenih usluga u školama tokom poslijekovidnog perioda, štetu po formiranje ljudskog kapitala koja pogoršava nezaposlenost mladih i emigraciju, te negativne efekte na povjerenje investitora i investicionu klimu u turizmu.

DEBATA PO POSLANIČKIM GRUPAMA

Blok DF/DNP (Demokratski front / Demokratska narodna partija) otvorio je opozicione intervencije odlučnom osudom. Blok je hapšenje okarakterisao kao političko proganjanje ministarke čiji je jedini prestup bio hrabrost da razmontira duboko ukorijenjeni DPS aparat u obrazovnom sistemu. DNP je izjavio da, ako razmontiranje institucionalnih mreža izgrađenih u toku trideset godina jednopartijske vladavine predstavlja krivično djelo, onda svi građani koji su glasali za promjene 30. avgusta 2020. također trebaju biti uhapšeni. Blok je skrenuo pažnju na dramatičan način hapšenja, tvrdeći da je prikazivanje profesorice u lisicama kao da je opasan kriminalac namjerna spektakl osmišljen da ponizi i zastraši. Predsjednik Skupštine Mandić, blizak ovom bloku, javno je izjavio da se klanovi, zelenaši i kriminalci smiješe fotografijama uhapšene profesorice i upozorio da oni koji nepromišljeno stavljaju lisice drugima sami mogu doživjeti isti tretman. Blok DF/DNP pokrenuo je i etničku dimenziju, napominjući da su saveznici u srpskom parlamentu gonjenje okarakterisali kao proganjanje osobe zbog toga što je Srpkinja i vjernica Srpske pravoslavne crkve. Blok je zahtijevao trenutno oslobađanje Bratić i pozvao Specijalno državno tužilaštvo da odgovori na pitanja o selektivnoj primjeni zakona.

Opozicija predvođena DPS-om (Demokratska partija socijalista i Socijaldemokratska partija) zauzela je primjetno drugačiji stav od bloka DF/DNP. Kao stranka čiji su kadrovi bili među onima koje je Bratić razriješila 2021. godine, DPS je gonjenje doživjela kao dugo čekanu potvrdu da su masovna razrješenja bila nezakonita. Međutim, DPS je također kritikovao postupak hapšenja kao pretjeran i doveo u pitanje vremenski okvir, pitajući zašto je Specijalnom državnom tužilaštvu trebalo tri godine od prijave CGO-a da preduzme mjere. Blok DPS-SDP tvrdio je da vladajuća koalicija pokušava instrumentalizovati gonjenje kako bi Briselu prikazala sliku vladavine prava, istovremeno tolišući političko miješanje od figura poput predsjednika Skupštine Mandića koji otvoreno vrši pritisak na pravosuđe. Opozicioni blok ukazao je na širi obrazac: ako je vlada ozbiljna u pogledu odgovornosti, treba objasniti zašto uporedivi ili ozbiljniji slučajevi zloupotrebe službenog položaja iz drugih vlada nisu dobili sličnu tužilačku pažnju. DPS je tvrdio da istinski antikorupcijski napredak zahtijeva dosljedno, neselektivno primjenjivanje zakona na sve političke opcije, a ne teatralna hapšenja koja zgodno ciljaju samo političke protivnike sadašnje konstelacije.

Vladajuća koalicija Evropa sad!/URA branila je institucionalni okvir pažljivo se distancirajući od specifičnosti postupka hapšenja. Koalicija je tvrdila da je gonjenje nezavisna mjera Specijalnog državnog tužilaštva, a ne vladina odluka, i da politički akteri sa svih strana moraju poštovati nezavisnost tužilaštva kako je Delegacija EU izričito zahtijevala. Koalicija je pozvala na ocjene ministarstava, posebno EU integracija i pravde, koje potvrđuju da gonjenje unapređuje crnogorski učinak u okviru Poglavlja 23. Po pitanju srazmjernosti, koalicija je priznala da postupci hapšenja treba da budu usklađeni s prirodom krivičnog djela i profilom rizika osumnjičenog, i izrazila podršku preporuci Ministarstva unutrašnjih poslova za razvoj formalnih operativnih smjernica. Koalicija je naglasila da crnogorski pristupni rok EU zahtijeva da se svi politički akteri fokusiraju na strukturne reforme umjesto da svaki sudski postupak pretvaraju u stranački sukob. Blok Evropa sad! pozvao se na ocjenu Ministarstva finansija koja pokazuje 4 miliona EUR troškova neuspješnog upravljanja od 2020. i tvrdio da ovaj obrazac fiskalne štete od politizovanih kadrovskih odluka mora biti okončan sistemskim zaštitnim mjerama, a ne stranačkim međusobnim optuživanjem.

Bošnjačka stranka i stranke albanskih manjina intervenisale su kako bi debatu usmjerile prema institucionalnim i ljudskopravaskim dimenzijama. Ove stranke napomenule su da masovno razrješenje 140 direktora škola 2021. nije bilo samo politički spor, već čin koji je poremetio obrazovne usluge za hiljade djece, nesrazmjerno pogađajući manjinske zajednice u opštinama s mješovitim stanovništvom. Manjinski blok podržao je princip odgovornosti i podržao preporuku Ministarstva prosvjete da se nadležnost imenovanja direktora prenese na školske odbore s predstavnicima roditelja, nastavnika i opštine, što bi bolje zaštitilo manjinsku zastupljenost u upravljanju školama. Što se tiče etničkog okvira koji promovišu DF/DNP, manjinske stranke odbacile su narativ da se Bratić goni zbog njene etničke pripadnosti, napominjući da takvo uokvirivanje instrumentalizuje identitetsku politiku kako bi se zvaničnici zaštitili od legitimne pravne odgovornosti. Manjinski blok pozvao se na analizu CdM-a da Bratić nije na suđenju zbog toga što je Srpkinja, i napomenuo da rukovodstvo Specijalnog državnog tužilaštva samo po sebi podriva tvrdnje o antisrpskoj pristranosti. Ove stranke pozvale su da debata bude usmjerena na institucionalnu reformu i obrazovna prava djece, a ne na etničku mobilizaciju.

TAČKE KONVERGENCIJE

Uprkos oštrim neslaganjima oko političke interpretacije hapšenja, tokom debate pojavilo se nekoliko tačaka međustranačkog slaganja.

Prvo, nijedan poslanički blok nije branio suštinu Bratićinih postupaka iz 2021. Čak i blok DF/DNP, koji je razmontiranje DPS aparata okarakterisao kao nužno, nije tvrdio da je specifičan pravni postupak korišćen za razrješenja bio ispravan. Sudski nalazi o nezakonitosti nisu bili osporeni ni od jedne parlamentarne grupacije.

Drugo, postojalo je široko slaganje da je postupak hapšenja bio nesrazmjeran. Ova kritika dolazila je sa svih strana političkog spektra -- od bloka DF/DNP koji ga je nazvao ponižavajućim, od DPS-a koji ga je nazvao pretjeranim, do vladajuće koalicije koja je priznala procesne nedostatke. Preporuka Ministarstva unutrašnjih poslova za formalne smjernice o srazmjernosti dobila je međustranačku podršku.

Treće, svi blokovi izrazili su principijelnu podršku sistemskim reformama radi sprečavanja budućih politički motivisanih masovnih razrješenja u javnom sektoru. Preporuka Ministarstva prosvjete da se nadležnost imenovanja direktora prenese s ministra na školske odbore citirane je s odobravanjem u svim dijelovima političkog spektra, mada su se stranke razlikovale po detaljima implementacije.

Četvrto, postojala je saglasnost da trogodišnje kašnjenje između prijave i hapšenja predstavlja problem i da Specijalno državno tužilaštvo mora transparentno objasniti vremenski okvir.

TAČKE SPORENJA

Glavno neslaganje ticalo se pitanja da li je gonjenje nepristrasno ili selektivno. Blok DF/DNP insistira da se radi o etnički i politički motivisanom proganjanju. DPS opozicija dovodi u pitanje da li je ono zaista neselektivno, s obzirom na odsustvo uporedivih gonjenja zvaničnika iz drugih vlada. Vladajuća koalicija tvrdi da se radi o nezavisnoj tužilačkoj odluci koja treba biti ocjenjivana na osnovu pravnih zasluga.

Etnicizacija slučaja predstavlja duboku liniju podjele. Blok DF/DNP i saveznici iz Beograda okviruju gonjenje kao antisrpsko proganjanje, dok manjinske stranke i vladajuća koalicija odbacuju ovaj okvir kao instrumentalizaciju identiteta u cilju ometanja odgovornosti. Ministarstvo evropskih integracija upozorilo je da ova dinamika direktno prijeti političkoj stabilnosti i reformskom konsenzusu neophodnom za pristupanje EU.

Uloga predsjednika Skupštine Mandića pojavila se kao sporna tema. Ministarstvo evropskih integracija izričito je upozorilo da Mandićeva javna kritika hapšenja prijeti da bude protumačena od Brisela kao politički pritisak na nezavisnost tužilaštva. DPS opozicija zahtijevala je odgovornost za ono što opisuje kao otvoreno miješanje predsjednika Skupštine u sudske postupke. Blok DF/DNP branio je Mandićeve izjave kao legitimni politički komentar i slobodu izražavanja.

PROCJENA NACIONALNOG INTERESA

Skupština konstatuje da se Crna Gora nalazi na ključnoj prekretnici u svom procesu pristupanja EU, s ambicijom da do kraja 2026. zatvori sva poglavlja pregovora. U tom kontekstu, gonjenje bivše ministarke zbog dokumentovane zloupotrebe službenog položaja služi strateškom interesu Crne Gore u izgradnji vjerodostojnog antikorupcijskog učinka koji Brisel zahtijeva. Međutim, ova korist uslovljena je vođenjem gonjenja na pravnim zaslugama, bez političkog miješanja i sa procesnim standardima koji bi izdržali provjeru Evropskog suda za ljudska prava.

Fiskalna dimenzija je značajna, ali sekundarna. Dokumentovana naknada od 280.000 EUR, koja će vjerovatno rasti kako se preostali slučajevi rješavaju, predstavlja izbjegivo rasipanje koje je moglo finansirati unaprjeđenja u obrazovanju. Identifikovanje od strane Ministarstva finansija šireg obrasca troškova neuspješnog upravljanja od 4 miliona EUR od 2020. naglašava potrebu za sistemskim fiskalnim zaštitnim mjerama.

Institucionalna šteta nanesena obrazovnom sistemu najdugotrajnija je posljedica. Više od 140 škola doživjelo je poremećaj rukovodstva tokom kritičnog perioda poslijekovidnog oporavka. Ocjena Ministarstva prosvjete da sistemska ranjivost ostaje -- jer ministar i dalje ima nadležnost za razrješenje direktora -- znači da bez zakonodavne reforme ovu epizodu može ponoviti bilo koja buduća vlada.

Politička polarizacija izazvana hapšenjem predstavlja stvarni rizik za upravljačku stabilnost Crne Gore i napredak u pristupanju EU. Etnicizacija onoga što je u osnovi slučaj vladavine prava prijeti produbljivanju društvenih podjela i daje vanjskim akterima polugu za ometanje euroatlantskih integracija Crne Gore.

UKUPNA OCJENA

Skupština utvrđuje da je gonjenje bivše ministrarke Bratić, u cjelini, pozitivan razvoj događaja za odgovornost i vladavinu prava u Crnoj Gori. Četiri ministarstva ocijenila su odluku pozitivno (finansije, prosvjeta, evropske integracije, pravda), a pet neutralno (ekologija, turizam, ekonomija, zdravlje, unutrašnji poslovi), dok ni jedno ministarstvo nije dalo negativnu ocjenu. Ukupna ocjena je pozitivna i odražava sljedeći balans: osnovno ponašanje je bilo jasno nezakonito i nanijelo je dokumentovanu štetu; odgovornost bivših ministara neophodna je za pristupanje EU i upravljački kredibilitet; međutim, mora se razriješiti pitanje srazmjernosti postupka hapšenja, gonjenje mora demonstrirati neselektivnost primjenjujući jednake standarde prema zvaničnicima svih političkih pozadina, a moraju se usvojiti sistemske reforme radi sprečavanja ponavljanja. Skupština poziva sve političke aktere da poštuju nezavisnost tužilaštva, da se uzdrže od etnicizacije sudskih postupaka i da daju prioritet zakonodavnim reformama koje preporučuju ministarstva prosvjete, pravde, unutrašnjih poslova i finansija, kako bi ova epizoda rezultirala trajnim institucionalnim unaprjeđenjem, a ne tek još jednim poglavljem u crnogorskom ciklusu političke odmazde.

blind_spots: President Milatovic's extraordinary intervention requesting a pardon for Bratic before any conviction -- arguably the most damaging development for Chapter 23 compliance. A sitting president pressuring the Ministry of Justice to initiate pardon proceedings for someone merely charged, not convicted, constitutes textbook political interference in the judicial process. No ministry flagged this despite it occurring on February 20, days after the arrest.

The actual scale of dismissals was far larger than reported. The Centre for Civic Education documented that 223 of 243 public educational institutions had directors replaced -- not just '140' as stated in the decision summary. This includes 158 of 169 primary school directors, 40 of 47 secondary directors, and 25 of 27 other institution directors. The ministries accepted the lower figure uncritically.

Cross-party prosecution pattern undermines the selective justice narrative. In December 2024, the same Special State Prosecutor's Office arrested two former DPS-era agriculture ministers (Milutin Simovic and Petar Ivanovic) on abuse of office charges involving over EUR 300,000. Combined with the simultaneous Saranovic arrest (a DPS-era Property Administration appointee accused of EUR 9 million in damages), the SPO is clearly prosecuting officials from both sides of the political divide. No ministry adequately contextualized this to counter the selective prosecution claim.

The role of Belgrade media and the Serbian Orthodox Church network in amplifying the ethnic framing of the case was not analyzed. Multiple sources report coordinated mobilization to portray the arrest as anti-Serbian persecution, which has geopolitical implications for Montenegro's EU trajectory and sovereignty. This external influence dimension is absent from all assessments.

No ministry examined whether Montenegro's education quality outcomes (PISA scores, graduation rates, teacher retention) were measurably affected during the 2021-2022 disruption period. The institutional damage is asserted but not quantified in educational outcome terms.

The ambitious timeline of closing all remaining EU negotiation chapters by end of 2026 was not assessed against the political crisis this case has triggered. Montenegro closed Chapter 32 in January 2026 and aims to close 20 remaining chapters -- political instability from this case could directly derail that schedule.

No ministry noted that the Higher Court's replacement of detention with house arrest and electronic monitoring happened quickly, which actually demonstrates judicial checks functioning properly -- a positive Chapter 23 indicator that was overlooked amid the focus on the initial overreach.
critic_report.overall_analysis:
The prosecution of Vesna Bratic is legally justified and substantively necessary. Courts have already established, across dozens of proceedings, that her mass dismissal of school directors was unlawful and that her own ministry sabotaged the process it cited as justification. The Centre for Civic Education documented that 223 of 243 public educational institutions were affected -- a wholesale political purge unprecedented in Montenegro's education system. The financial damage exceeds EUR 280,000 in confirmed compensation with total exposure likely several times higher. Criminal accountability for a former minister who caused documented harm to the state is exactly what the EU demands under Chapters 23 and 24.

However, the execution of this accountability was badly botched. Deploying special police units with handcuffs for a non-violent administrative offense by a university professor with no flight risk was operationally indefensible. The Higher Court's swift correction -- replacing detention with house arrest and electronic monitoring -- confirms the initial overreach. This disproportionality handed Bratic's political allies a legitimate grievance that they have exploited to obscure the substance of the charges.

The most damaging development, which no ministry addressed, is President Milatovic's request on February 20 for the Ministry of Justice to initiate pardon proceedings for Bratic -- before any trial, let alone conviction. This is an extraordinary act of political interference in the judicial process. When a sitting president publicly pressures the justice system on behalf of a charged suspect, it sends precisely the signal the EU has warned against: that political connections can override legal processes. If Brussels takes note of this presidential intervention, it could do more damage to Montenegro's Chapter 23 progress than the arrest itself.

The selective justice narrative promoted by opposition parties is significantly weakened by the broader prosecution pattern. The same Special State Prosecutor's Office arrested two former DPS agriculture ministers (Simovic and Ivanovic) in December 2024 and simultaneously arrested Blazo Saranovic, a DPS-era Property Administration director accused of EUR 9 million in damages. The SPO is clearly pursuing officials from across the political spectrum. The ministries largely failed to contextualize this, allowing the selective prosecution claim to stand unchallenged.

The ethnicization of the case -- with coordinated mobilization through pro-Serbian parties, Belgrade media, and the Serbian Orthodox Church network to frame the prosecution as anti-Serbian persecution -- represents a serious threat to Montenegro's political stability and EU trajectory. This is not just a domestic legal matter; it has become a tool in the broader geopolitical contest over Montenegro's orientation. No ministry addressed this external influence dimension.

On fiscal impact, the EUR 280,000 figure is almost certainly an undercount. With 223 institutions affected and approximately 157 court proceedings documented by CGO, the total exposure including back pay, court costs, legal fees, and pending claims could reach EUR 1 million or more. This sits within a broader pattern: approximately EUR 4 million paid to former officials since 2020 in wrongful termination-related compensation, representing a systemic governance failure where each government transition triggers a cascade of political purges followed by costly litigation.

The structural problem remains unresolved. Despite September 2024 amendments to the General Law on Education, the minister retains the power to appoint and dismiss school directors. EU best practice in countries like Estonia and Finland places this authority with school boards or local governance bodies. Without this structural reform, the Bratic episode can recur under any future government.

The ministry assessments were generally strong. Education, Justice, Interior, and EU Integration produced genuinely analytical work. The Education Ministry's assessment was particularly impressive in its granular documentation and structural reform proposals. The Environment and Health ministries honestly acknowledged their lack of direct relevance. The Tourism ministry provided useful investment climate analysis. However, the collective failure to identify the Milatovic pardon intervention, the cross-party prosecution context, and the geopolitical dimension of ethnic mobilization represents meaningful blind spots.

Bottom line: Montenegro needs this prosecution to succeed on its merits. The underlying conduct was clearly unlawful and harmful. But the disproportionate arrest, the presidential pardon request, and the ethnicization of the case have transformed what should have been a straightforward accountability exercise into a political crisis that risks setting back EU accession progress. The institution that should be most concerned is not the SPO but the Presidency -- Milatovic's intervention is far more corrosive to rule of law than any procedural misstep by police.
critic_report.headline: Right Case, Wrong Arrest, Presidential Pardon Plea Makes It Worse
eu_chapter_relevance: Ch.23 Judiciary and Fundamental Rights -- The prosecution of a former minister for court-confirmed unlawful conduct directly tests Montenegro's anti-corruption track record required for final benchmarks. However, President Milatovic's pardon request before any conviction constitutes political interference in the judicial process, and Parliament Speaker Mandic's public criticism risks being flagged by Brussels as pressure on prosecutorial independence. The disproportionate arrest procedure may raise ECHR Article 5 proportionality concerns.

Ch.24 Justice, Freedom and Security -- The Special State Prosecutor's Office capacity to handle multiple simultaneous high-profile cases (Bratic, Saranovic, former agriculture ministers) is being tested against documented resource and infrastructure constraints noted in the 2025 EU Progress Report. The political backlash and ethnicization of prosecutions could undermine the cross-party consensus needed for security sector reforms.

Ch.19 Social Policy and Employment -- The mass dismissal of 223 school directors without due process violated merit-based public employment principles required under this chapter. The pattern of politically motivated personnel purges with each government transition represents a systemic governance failure that contradicts EU standards on stable, professional public administration.

Ch.26 Education and Culture -- Although provisionally closed, the underlying conduct exposed structural weaknesses in education governance. The minister's unreformed power to appoint and dismiss school directors contradicts EU best practice and risks reopening questions about institutional safeguards in the education sector.

Ch.33 Financial and Budgetary Provisions -- Over EUR 280,000 confirmed in compensation payments (with total exposure potentially reaching EUR 1 million) diverted from the education budget represents avoidable fiscal waste. This sits within a broader EUR 4 million pattern of wrongful termination compensation since 2020, indicating systemic budgetary risk from politicized personnel management.
critic_report.headline_mne: Pravi slučaj, pogrešno hapšenje, predsjednički zahtjev za pomilovanje ga čini još gorim
critic_report.overall_analysis_mne:
Krivično gonjenje Vesne Bratić pravno je opravdano i suštinski neophodno. Sudovi su već utvrdili, u desetinama postupaka, da je masovno razrješenje direktora škola koje je sprovela bilo nezakonito i da je njeno ministarstvo sabotiralo postupak koji je navela kao opravdanje. Centar za građansko obrazovanje dokumentovao je da je pogođeno 223 od 243 javnih obrazovnih ustanova – sveobuhvatna politička čistka bez presedana u crnogorskom obrazovnom sistemu. Finansijska šteta premašuje 280.000 EUR u potvrđenim naknadama, a ukupna izloženost vjerovatno je nekoliko puta veća. Krivična odgovornost bivše ministrarke koja je nanijela dokumentovanu štetu državi upravo je ono što EU zahtijeva u okviru poglavlja 23 i 24.

Međutim, sprovođenje ove odgovornosti bilo je ozbiljno pogrešno. Angažovanje specijalnih policijskih jedinica s lisicama zbog nenasilnog administrativnog prekršaja od strane profesorice univerziteta bez rizika od bijega bilo je operativno neopravdivo. Brza korekcija Višeg suda – zamjena pritvora kućnim pritvorom i elektronskim nadzorom – potvrđuje početno prekoračenje ovlašćenja. Ova nesrazmjernost dala je političkim saveznicima Bratić legitimno oruđe koje su iskoristili kako bi zamutili suštinu optužbi.

Najštetniji razvoj događaja, koji nijedno ministarstvo nije naslovilo, jeste zahtjev predsjednika Milatovića od 20. februara upućen Ministarstvu pravde da pokrene postupak pomilovanja Bratić – prije ikakavog suđenja, a kamoli presude. Ovo je vanredan čin političkog miješanja u sudski proces. Kada sjedeći predsjednik javno vrši pritisak na pravosuđe u korist optužene osobe, šalje upravo onaj signal pred kojim EU upozorava: da politička povezanost može nadjačati zakonske procese. Ako Brisel uzme u obzir ovu predsjedničku intervenciju, ona bi mogla nanijeti više štete napretku Crne Gore u poglavlju 23 nego samo hapšenje.

Narativ o selektivnoj pravdi koji promovišu opozicione stranke znatno je oslabljen širim obrascem krivičnog gonjenja. Isti Specijalni državni tužilac uhapsio je u decembru 2024. dvojicu bivših DPS-ovih ministara poljoprivrede (Simovića i Ivanovića) i istovremeno uhapsio Blaža Šaranovića, direktora Uprave za imovinu iz DPS-ovog doba, optuženog za štetu od 9 miliona EUR. Jasno je da SDT goni funkcionere iz cijelog političkog spektra. Ministarstva su uglavnom propustila da ovo kontekstualizuju, dopuštajući da tvrdnja o selektivnom gonjenju ostane neosporena.

Etnicizacija slučaja – uz koordinisanu mobilizaciju putem prosrpskih stranaka, beogradskih medija i mreže Srpske pravoslavne crkve s ciljem da se gonjenje okviri kao antisrpski progon – predstavlja ozbiljnu prijetnju političkoj stabilnosti Crne Gore i njenom EU putu. Ovo nije samo domaće pravno pitanje; postalo je oruđe u širem geopolitičkom sukobu oko orijentacije Crne Gore. Nijedno ministarstvo nije se bavilo ovom dimenzijom vanjskog uticaja.

Što se tiče fiskalnog uticaja, iznos od 280.000 EUR gotovo sigurno je podcijenjen. S obzirom na to da je pogođeno 223 ustanove i da je CGO dokumentovao oko 157 sudskih postupaka, ukupna izloženost uključujući zaostale plate, sudske troškove, advokatske naknade i neriješene zahtjeve mogla bi dosegnuti 1 milion EUR ili više. To je dio šireg obrasca: otprilike 4 miliona EUR isplaćeno bivšim funkcionerima od 2020. godine po osnovu naknada za nezakonit otkaz, što predstavlja sistemski propust upravljanja u kome svaka smjena vlasti izaziva kaskadu političkih čistki praćenih skupim sporovima.

Strukturni problem ostaje neriješen. Uprkos izmjenama Opšteg zakona o obrazovanju iz septembra 2024, ministar zadržava ovlašćenje da imenuje i razrješava direktore škola. Dobra praksa EU u zemljama poput Estonije i Finske ovo ovlašćenje povjerava školskim odborima ili organima lokalne uprave. Bez ove strukturne reforme, epizoda poput Bratićeve može se ponoviti pod bilo kojom budućom vladom.

Ocjene ministarstava bile su generalno snažne. Ministarstva prosvjete, pravde, unutrašnjih poslova i evropskih integracija dala su zaista analitičke doprinose. Ocjena Ministarstva prosvjete bila je posebno impresivna po granularnoj dokumentaciji i prijedlozima strukturnih reformi. Ministarstva životne sredine i zdravlja pošteno su priznala nedostatak direktne relevantnosti. Ministarstvo turizma pružilo je korisnu analizu investicione klime. Međutim, kolektivni propust da se identifikuje intervencija predsjednika Milatovića u vidu zahtjeva za pomilovanje, kontekst krivičnog gonjenja svih stranaka i geopolitička dimenzija etničke mobilizacije predstavljaju značajne slijepe tačke.

Zaključak: Crnoj Gori je potrebno da ovo krivično gonjenje uspije na osnovu svojih merita. Osnovno postupanje bilo je jasno nezakonito i štetno. Međutim, nesrazmjerno hapšenje, predsjednički zahtjev za pomilovanje i etnicizacija slučaja pretvorili su ono što je trebalo biti jednostavna vježba odgovornosti u političku krizu koja prijeti da uspori napredak u pristupanju EU. Institucija koja bi trebala biti najzabrinutija nije SDT, već Predsjedništvo – Milatovićeva intervencija daleko je štetnije po vladavinu prava od bilo kojeg proceduralnog propusta policije.
blind_spots_mne: Vanredna intervencija predsjednika Milatovića kojom zahtijeva pomilovanje Bratić prije ikakve presude – što je vjerovatno najštetniji razvoj događaja za usklađenost s poglavljem 23. Sjedeći predsjednik koji vrši pritisak na Ministarstvo pravde da pokrene postupak pomilovanja za osobu koja je tek optužena, a ne osuđena, predstavlja udžbenički primjer političkog miješanja u sudski proces. Nijedno ministarstvo nije ukazalo na ovo, iako se dogodilo 20. februara, nekoliko dana nakon hapšenja.

Stvarni obim razrješenja bio je daleko veći nego što je prijavljeno. Centar za građansko obrazovanje dokumentovao je da je u 223 od 243 javnih obrazovnih ustanova zamijenjen direktor – a ne samo u '140' kako je navedeno u rezimeu odluke. To uključuje 158 od 169 direktora osnovnih škola, 40 od 47 direktora srednjih škola i 25 od 27 direktora ostalih ustanova. Ministarstva su nekritički prihvatila niži broj.

Obrazac krivičnog gonjenja koji obuhvata sve stranke potkopava narativ o selektivnoj pravdi. U decembru 2024, isti Specijalni državni tužilac uhapsio je dvojicu bivših DPS-ovih ministara poljoprivrede (Milutina Simovića i Petra Ivanovića) po optužbama za zloupotrebu položaja u iznosu od preko 300.000 EUR. U kombinaciji sa istovremenim hapšenjem Šaranovića (funkcionera Uprave za imovinu iz DPS-ovog doba, optuženog za štetu od 9 miliona EUR), jasno je da SDT goni funkcionere s obje strane političke podjele. Nijedno ministarstvo nije adekvatno kontekstualizovalo ovo kako bi osporilo tvrdnju o selektivnom gonjenju.

Uloga beogradskih medija i mreže Srpske pravoslavne crkve u pojačavanju etničkog okvira slučaja nije analizirana. Više izvora izvještava o koordinisanoj mobilizaciji s ciljem predstavljanja hapšenja kao antisrpskog progona, što ima geopolitičke implikacije za EU put Crne Gore i njenu suverenost. Ova dimenzija vanjskog uticaja odsutna je iz svih ocjena.

Nijedno ministarstvo nije ispitalo da li su obrazovni ishodi Crne Gore (PISA rezultati, stope završetka školovanja, zadržavanje nastavnika) bili mjerljivo ugroženi tokom perioda poremećaja 2021–2022. Institucionalna šteta tvrdi se, ali nije kvantifikovana u smislu obrazovnih ishoda.

Ambiciozni vremenski okvir zatvaranja svih preostalih pregovaračkih poglavlja EU do kraja 2026. nije procjenjivan u kontekstu političke krize koju je ovaj slučaj pokrenuo. Crna Gora je zatvorila poglavlje 32 u januaru 2026. i planira zatvaranje 20 preostalih poglavlja – politička nestabilnost izazvana ovim slučajem mogla bi direktno ugroziti taj raspored.

Nijedno ministarstvo nije napomenulo da je Viši sud brzo zamijenio pritvor kućnim pritvorom i elektronskim nadzorom, što zapravo dokazuje da pravosudne provjere funkcionišu pravilno – pozitivan pokazatelj za poglavlje 23 koji je bio previđen usred fokusa na početno prekoračenje ovlašćenja.

counter_proposal.title: Preventive School Governance Reform and Proportionate Accountability Act
counter_proposal.executive_summary: Rather than relying solely on a heavy-handed criminal arrest to address the consequences of the 2021 mass dismissal of school principals, the AI Government of Montenegro would pursue a dual-track approach: first, ensure the ongoing prosecution proceeds proportionately and on legal merit using summons-based procedures and parallel civil recovery of state funds; second, enact comprehensive structural reforms that transfer school director appointment authority to autonomous school boards, mandate fiscal and service-delivery impact assessments before mass personnel decisions, and establish an independent Education Governance Commission -- making it structurally impossible for any future minister to repeat such abuses. This approach addresses the root causes of the Bratic episode rather than just its symptoms, while strengthening Montenegro's Chapter 23 and 24 final benchmark compliance ahead of the Q3 2026 deadline.
counter_proposal.detailed_proposal:
The arrest of former Education Minister Vesna Bratic exposes two distinct failures in Montenegrin governance: first, the absence of structural safeguards that allowed a single minister to unlawfully dismiss over 140 school principals in 2021; second, the lack of proportionality guidelines in law enforcement that turned a legitimate prosecution into a political crisis. This counter-proposal addresses both failures through a comprehensive reform package grounded in EU best practices and Montenegro's own accession commitments.

TRACK ONE: PROPORTIONATE PROSECUTION AND FISCAL RECOVERY

The criminal investigation should continue on its merits. Courts have already confirmed the unlawfulness of the dismissals in over 130 proceedings, and the Special State Prosecutor's Office has a sound legal basis under Article 416 of the Criminal Code. However, the manner of prosecution must change. For non-violent abuse-of-office cases where the suspect is a former official with no documented flight risk, court summons should be the default procedure, with a 48-hour compliance window before escalation to warrant-based arrest. This aligns with European Court of Human Rights jurisprudence on proportionality under Article 5 of the ECHR, and the Higher Court's own subsequent decision to replace detention with house arrest and electronic monitoring confirms the initial overreach.

The government should immediately initiate civil recovery proceedings through the State Attorney to recoup the EUR 280,000-plus already paid in compensation, plus ongoing liabilities that multiple sources estimate could reach EUR 500,000 to EUR 1 million once all 157 court proceedings conclude. A dedicated Fiscal Recovery Unit within the Ministry of Finance, staffed through reallocation of existing legal personnel, should be tasked with pursuing civil claims against officials whose unlawful actions result in documented state budget losses. This mechanism would apply not only to the Bratic case but to the broader pattern of politically motivated dismissals that the Centre for Civic Education estimates has cost approximately EUR 4 million since 2020.

All political officeholders, including the Parliament Speaker, should adhere to a formal Government protocol requiring standardized statements of non-interference when prosecutorial actions target former officials. The EU Delegation to Montenegro has explicitly warned against political statements that could be interpreted as influencing prosecutorial decisions. Such a protocol would provide documented evidence for Chapter 23 final benchmark assessment that Montenegro protects prosecutorial autonomy.

TRACK TWO: SCHOOL GOVERNANCE INDEPENDENCE REFORM

The core structural reform is the transfer of school director appointment and dismissal authority from the minister to school boards. This is the single most important change, because without it, the Bratic episode can recur under any future government. The current legal framework, even after the September 2024 amendments to the General Law on Education, still vests the minister with the power to elect and dismiss school directors.

Under the proposed reform, school boards composed of elected parent representatives, teacher representatives, and municipal delegates would conduct open competitions for director positions based on published criteria and scored evaluations. The minister would retain a supervisory role limited to legal compliance review, with veto power only for documented procedural violations. Serbia attempted a similar reform through its school board model (three school representatives, three parents, three local community representatives), though implementation challenges there -- where municipalities controlled board composition -- provide a cautionary lesson. Montenegro's model must ensure that no single political actor controls board membership.

An independent Education Governance Commission, with three members nominated respectively by Parliament, teacher unions, and parent associations, would oversee appointment processes, adjudicate disputes, and have the power to annul irregular appointments or dismissals. This body provides the institutional check that was entirely absent in 2021. Mandatory qualification standards for school directors -- including minimum management training, pedagogical experience, and professional certification -- would further insulate positions from purely political appointments, as the Centre for Civic Education documented that some replacement acting directors appointed in 2021 lacked required qualifications.

A statutory protection period of 18 months following any change of government, during which director positions cannot be altered except through documented performance-based cause adjudicated by the Education Governance Commission, would prevent the wholesale political purges that have characterized government transitions in Montenegro. This cooling-off mechanism addresses the cyclical pattern where each new government attempts to replace the previous government's appointees.

TRACK THREE: CROSS-SECTORAL MASS PERSONNEL DECISION SAFEGUARDS

The Bratic case revealed a governance gap that extends beyond education. No mechanism exists to prevent any minister from making mass personnel decisions that generate enormous fiscal liabilities without prior review. The counter-proposal establishes a mandatory dual-review requirement for any administrative decision affecting the leadership of 10 or more public institutions simultaneously.

First, a fiscal impact assessment signed off by the Ministry of Finance, quantifying potential compensation costs, legal fees, and institutional disruption expenses. Second, a service-delivery impact assessment evaluating effects on beneficiaries, including health services in schools, psychosocial support programmes, and special education provisions. These assessments would be required before any mass personnel decision takes effect, creating an institutional firewall against impulsive political purges.

The review mechanism can be implemented through amendment to the existing Law on State Administration, drawing on the European Commission's existing recommendation that Montenegro adopt a Law on Government with disciplinary penalties. Quarterly reporting by each ministry on pending and resolved legal disputes arising from administrative decisions, including cumulative fiscal exposure estimates, would provide transparency and early warning of governance failures.

TRACK FOUR: EU ACCESSION ALIGNMENT

All proposed reforms are designed to maximize Montenegro's compliance with Chapters 23 and 24 final benchmarks ahead of the Q3 2026 deadline. The European Commission's 2025 Progress Report noted that Montenegro's track record on high-level corruption investigations has improved but final convictions remain low. This counter-proposal converts the Bratic case from a potentially problematic precedent (disproportionate arrest procedure, political backlash, risk of ECHR challenge) into evidence of systemic reform capacity.

An inter-institutional working group linking the Special State Prosecutor's Office, Ministry of Justice, and Ministry of EU Integration should systematically document accountability outcomes and feed them into final benchmark reporting. IPA III technical assistance funds should be allocated specifically to strengthen SPO capacity for complex high-level cases, addressing the European Commission's concern about insufficient human resources and infrastructure at the SPO. The EEAS statement by Ambassador Sattler that it is time for swift and decisive action on Chapters 23 and 24 reinforces the urgency of demonstrating structural reform rather than relying on individual prosecutions.

COST AND FEASIBILITY

Most proposed changes involve procedural guidelines, institutional protocols, and amendments to existing legislation already in the EU accession pipeline. The fiscal impact screening mechanism and proportionality guidelines for arrest procedures can be implemented by government decree within 3-6 months. The Education Governance Commission and school board reform require legislative amendment to the General Law on Education, achievable within 12-18 months given that the September 2024 amendments demonstrate parliamentary capacity for education legislation. The Fiscal Recovery Unit requires reallocation of existing legal staff within the Ministry of Finance, achievable within one budget cycle. Total direct implementation costs are minimal, primarily staff time for drafting and training, with EU TAIEX or Council of Europe technical assistance available for the proportionality guidelines and school governance reform. The potential savings are substantial: preventing even one future episode comparable to the Bratic dismissals would save the state budget an estimated EUR 500,000 to EUR 1 million in avoided compensation costs.
ministry_contributions: Ministry of Education: Provided the backbone proposal for school governance reform, including transfer of director appointment authority to school boards, the Education Governance Commission concept, mandatory qualification standards, and the 18-month statutory cooling-off period following government changes.

Ministry of Justice: Contributed the proportionality framework for arrest procedures, including summons-first protocol for non-violent offenses, and the analysis of ECHR Article 5 compliance risks that could undermine the prosecution at the European Court level.

Ministry of Interior: Reinforced proportionality concerns with operational detail on arrest procedure guidelines, mandatory Proportionality Assessment Forms for police commanders, and the joint SDT-Police protocol for risk-based suspect classification.

Ministry of Finance: Contributed the Fiscal Recovery Unit concept, mandatory fiscal impact screening for mass personnel decisions, personal financial liability clauses for officials, and quarterly fiscal exposure reporting requirements.

Ministry of Economy and Labour: Provided the mandatory independent administrative review mechanism for mass dismissals exceeding 10 positions, the Professional Standards Board concept adapted for the Education Governance Commission, and the public sector employment stability index.

Ministry of EU Integration: Contributed the EU accession alignment framework, including the inter-institutional working group for benchmark reporting, the formal non-interference protocol for political officeholders, IPA III technical assistance allocation for SPO capacity, and the civil recovery proceedings as a parallel accountability track.

Ministry of Health: Contributed the service-delivery impact assessment requirement, particularly regarding the disruption to school-based health services and psychosocial support during mass personnel transitions, and the concept of institutional firewalls protecting vulnerable populations.

Ministry of Tourism, Ecology, Sustainable Development and Northern Region Development: Contributed the perspective that governance credibility should be demonstrated through concrete delivery on policy commitments rather than high-profile arrests, and the link between educational disruption in the northern region and sustainable development workforce deficits.
key_differences: The original decision relied solely on ex-post criminal prosecution with a disproportionate arrest procedure. This counter-proposal adds preventive structural reforms that make future mass political purges of school directors structurally impossible, regardless of which party holds government.

The original decision treated this as a criminal justice matter. This counter-proposal treats it as a systemic governance failure requiring legislative reform to the General Law on Education, transferring director appointment authority from the minister to autonomous school boards.

The original decision generated political backlash that risks undermining its own rule-of-law value. This counter-proposal establishes formal non-interference protocols and proportionality guidelines that protect prosecutorial independence while meeting ECHR standards, converting a politically toxic episode into credible Chapter 23 evidence.

The original decision made no provision for recovering the EUR 280,000-plus already paid in compensation. This counter-proposal creates a dedicated civil recovery track through the State Attorney and a Fiscal Recovery Unit at the Ministry of Finance to recoup documented state losses.

The original decision addressed one former minister's conduct in isolation. This counter-proposal establishes cross-sectoral safeguards -- mandatory fiscal and service-delivery impact assessments for any mass personnel decision affecting 10 or more institutions -- that prevent similar abuses across all ministries, including health, interior, and other sectors.

The original decision offered no remedy for the institutional damage caused to 140-plus schools. This counter-proposal includes mandatory qualification standards for directors, merit-based selection through open competition, and an 18-month cooling-off period to restore and protect institutional continuity in education.
implementation_steps: Months 1-3: Issue Government decree establishing mandatory fiscal impact assessment for any administrative decision affecting 10 or more public sector leadership positions, requiring Ministry of Finance sign-off before implementation. This requires no legislative change and creates an immediate safeguard.

Months 1-3: Develop and adopt formal Operational Guidelines on Proportionate Use of Coercive Measures for police arrest procedures, specifying that summons is the default for non-violent abuse-of-office suspects with no documented flight risk. Use EU TAIEX technical assistance for drafting.

Months 1-3: Adopt Government protocol requiring standardized non-interference statements from political officeholders when prosecutorial actions target former officials, aligned with EU Delegation guidance for Chapter 23 compliance.

Months 1-6: Initiate civil recovery proceedings through the State Attorney against former Minister Bratic and any other officials whose unlawful actions resulted in the EUR 280,000-plus in documented state compensation payments.

Months 1-6: Establish a Fiscal Recovery Unit within the Ministry of Finance through reallocation of existing legal staff, tasked with identifying and pursuing civil claims for all state budget losses arising from unlawful administrative decisions since 2020.

Months 3-6: Form an inter-institutional working group linking the Special State Prosecutor's Office, Ministry of Justice, and Ministry of EU Integration to document accountability outcomes for Chapter 23/24 final benchmark reporting ahead of the Q3 2026 deadline.

Months 3-6: Request IPA III technical assistance reallocation to strengthen SPO capacity, including potential secondment of EU prosecutors as advisors, addressing the European Commission's concern about insufficient human resources.

Months 3-9: Draft amendments to the General Law on Education transferring school director appointment and dismissal authority from the minister to school boards composed of parent, teacher, and municipal representatives. Conduct public consultation including with the Centre for Civic Education, UNICEF Montenegro, and teacher unions.

Months 6-12: Establish the independent Education Governance Commission with three members nominated by Parliament, teacher unions, and parent associations, empowered to review and annul irregular appointments or dismissals.

Months 6-12: Adopt mandatory qualification standards for school directors including minimum management training, pedagogical experience, and professional certification requirements, with a two-year transition period for current directors to meet the standards.

Months 9-15: Enact the 18-month statutory cooling-off period following government changes, during which school director positions cannot be altered except through documented performance-based cause adjudicated by the Education Governance Commission.

Months 12-18: Commission independent audit of the educational impact of the 2021-2022 mass dismissals, including effects on student outcomes, teacher turnover, school-based health services, and institutional continuity, to inform ongoing governance reforms.

Months 12-18: Implement quarterly ministerial reporting on pending and resolved legal disputes arising from administrative decisions, including cumulative fiscal exposure estimates, with reports published and submitted to Parliament.

Months 6-12: Amend the Law on State Administration to require mandatory service-delivery impact assessments for mass personnel decisions, covering effects on health services, psychosocial support, and special education provisions in affected institutions.
risks_and_tradeoffs: Political resistance to school board reform is the primary risk. Transferring director appointment power away from the minister reduces patronage capacity, which parties across the political spectrum have exploited. Serbia's experience shows that even after formal transfer to school boards, political parties found ways to control board composition through municipal appointments. Montenegro's model must include safeguards against this, such as direct election of parent and teacher representatives, but political resistance may delay or water down the legislation.

The 18-month cooling-off period constrains legitimate governance. A new minister who identifies genuinely underperforming school directors would be unable to act quickly. The mitigation -- allowing removal for documented cause adjudicated by the Education Governance Commission -- adds a procedural step that may frustrate needed personnel changes. This is a deliberate tradeoff: accepting some loss of ministerial flexibility to prevent the far greater harm of mass political purges.

Civil recovery proceedings against Bratic may yield limited financial returns.
